cards.answer: Old Architecture: JavaScript and native code communicate via an asynchronous JSON bridge. JS sends serialized messages to native, native executes and sends results back. Problems: serialization overhead, asynchronous-only (can't synchronously read layout), and single-threaded bridge is a bottleneck. New Architecture (Fabric + JSI + TurboModules): JSI (JavaScript Interface) enables direct, synchronous calls between JS and C++ without serialization. Fabric: new rendering system that creates shadow tree in C++, enabling synchronous layout measurement. TurboModules: lazy-loaded native modules with type-safe codegen from TypeScript specs. Codegen ensures JS and native types match. Result: faster startup, smoother animations, and synchronous native access.

cards.question: How does Flutter's widget tree and rendering pipeline work?
cards.answer: Flutter has three trees: Widget tree (immutable configuration — what you declare), Element tree (mutable instances managing lifecycle — created from widgets), and RenderObject tree (handles layout and painting). When state changes: build() creates new Widget tree, framework diffs against Elements, updates only changed RenderObjects. Rendering pipeline: build → layout (constraints go down, sizes go up) → paint (to a layer tree) → compositing (GPU). Key optimizations: const constructors (skip rebuild), RepaintBoundary (isolate repaint regions), and Keys (help diffing algorithm match elements). setState() triggers rebuild of the subtree. For complex state: InheritedWidget, Provider, Riverpod, or BLoC pattern.

cards.question: What are Swift optionals and how do you safely unwrap them?
cards.answer: Optionals represent values that may be nil: `var name: String?`. Safe unwrapping: optional binding (`if let name = name { use(name) }` or `guard let name = name else { return }`), nil coalescing (`name ?? "default"`), optional chaining (`user?.address?.city` — returns nil if any part is nil). Unsafe: force unwrap (`name!`) crashes on nil — avoid in production. Implicitly unwrapped optionals (`var name: String!`) are used for late initialization (IBOutlets) where you guarantee a value exists after setup. map/flatMap on optionals: `name.map { $0.uppercased() }` returns Optional<String>. Optionals are enums under the hood: `.some(value)` or `.none`.

cards.question: How do you implement offline-first architecture in mobile apps?
cards.answer: Offline-first means the app works fully without network, syncing when connected. Local storage: SQLite (Room on Android, Core Data/GRDB on iOS), Realm, or Hive (Flutter). Sync strategies: optimistic updates (apply locally, queue for server), conflict resolution (last-write-wins, server-wins, or manual merge), and event sourcing (store operations, replay to reconcile). Implementation: local DB as source of truth, write operations go to local DB + sync queue, background sync worker pushes queued operations when online, pull remote changes and merge. Tools: WorkManager (Android), BGTaskScheduler (iOS) for background sync. CRDTs can handle automatic conflict resolution for collaborative data.

cards.question: What is Kotlin null safety and how does it prevent NullPointerException?
cards.answer: Kotlin's type system distinguishes nullable (String?) from non-nullable (String) types at compile time. You cannot assign null to a non-nullable type. For nullable types, you must handle null explicitly: safe call (`user?.name` — returns null if user is null), Elvis operator (`name ?: "default"` — provides fallback), safe cast (`obj as? String`), and let/run for scoping (`user?.let { sendEmail(it) }`). The !! operator force-asserts non-null (throws NPE if null — equivalent to Java's behavior). Smart casts: after a null check, the compiler automatically narrows the type: `if (name != null) { name.length }`. Platform types (Java interop) are treated as nullable by default.

cards.question: How do you handle navigation in mobile apps?
cards.answer: iOS: UINavigationController (push/pop stack), UITabBarController (tabs), present/dismiss (modals). SwiftUI: NavigationStack with navigationDestination, NavigationLink, and programmatic navigation via path. Android: Jetpack Navigation Component with NavGraph (XML or Kotlin DSL), NavController, and SafeArgs for type-safe argument passing. Compose: NavHost with composable() destinations. React Native: React Navigation (stack, tab, drawer navigators) with typed routes. Flutter: Navigator 2.0 (Router API) or go_router for declarative routing. All platforms handle: deep linking, back stack management, argument passing, and shared element transitions. Deep linking requires mapping URLs to navigation destinations.

cards.question: What are App Store and Play Store review guidelines you should know?
cards.answer: Apple App Store: no hidden functionality, no private API usage, clear privacy policy and App Tracking Transparency consent, in-app purchases required for digital content (30% commission, 15% for Small Business Program), no external payment links (with some exceptions for reader apps), performance must be acceptable (no crashes, no excessive battery drain), and apps must work on the latest SDK. Google Play Store: target API level requirements (latest within a year), permission justifications required, data safety section required, no misleading claims, proper use of foreground services, and compliance with Families Policy for children's content. Both: accessibility support, proper content ratings, and privacy compliance (GDPR, CCPA).

cards.question: Explain dependency injection in mobile apps (Hilt, Swinject).
cards.answer: Dependency injection provides objects their dependencies externally rather than creating them internally. Benefits: testability (inject mocks), loose coupling, and configuration flexibility. Android (Hilt/Dagger): annotation-based. @HiltAndroidApp on Application, @AndroidEntryPoint on Activities/Fragments, @Inject constructor for automatic injection, @Module + @Provides for third-party dependencies, @Singleton/@ViewModelScoped for scoping. iOS (Swinject/Factory): container.register/resolve pattern or property wrappers (@Injected). Swift also uses protocol-based DI (inject protocol, provide concrete implementation). Flutter: Provider/Riverpod (widget tree DI) or get_it (service locator). Constructor injection is preferred over service locator pattern for better compile-time safety.

cards.question: How do you test mobile apps? Compare unit, widget, and integration tests.
cards.answer: Unit tests: test individual functions/classes in isolation, fast, no UI framework needed. Mock dependencies. iOS: XCTest, Android: JUnit + Mockk, Flutter: test package. Widget/component tests: test UI components with simulated interactions, faster than full integration. iOS: XCTestUI with ViewInspector, Android: Compose Testing (ComposeTestRule), Flutter: flutter_test (pumpWidget, find, tap). Integration/E2E tests: test full app flows on real/emulated devices, slowest but most realistic. iOS: XCUITest, Android: Espresso/UIAutomator, Flutter: integration_test, Cross-platform: Detox, Appium, Maestro. Testing pyramid: many unit tests, fewer widget tests, fewer integration tests. Snapshot testing captures UI regressions visually.

cards.question: What is Grand Central Dispatch (GCD) in iOS?
cards.answer: GCD is Apple's concurrency framework that manages thread pools for you. Dispatch queues: main queue (serial, UI updates), global queues (concurrent, with QoS priorities: userInteractive, userInitiated, utility, background), and custom queues (serial or concurrent). Usage: `DispatchQueue.global().async { heavyWork(); DispatchQueue.main.async { updateUI() } }`. DispatchGroup coordinates multiple async tasks (notify when all complete). DispatchSemaphore limits concurrent access. DispatchWorkItem supports cancellation. Modern replacement: Swift Concurrency (async/await, actors, structured concurrency) provides safer, more readable code. Actors prevent data races by serializing access. Task groups replace DispatchGroup with structured cancellation.

cards.question: How does GraphQL differ from REST?
cards.answer: REST: multiple endpoints (GET /users, GET /users/1/posts), server defines response shape, over-fetching (get all fields even if you need one) and under-fetching (need multiple requests for related data). GraphQL: single endpoint, client specifies exactly what fields and relationships it needs in the query, gets exactly that back. One request can traverse relationships (user → posts → comments). GraphQL uses a typed schema (SDL) as a contract. Tradeoffs: GraphQL adds complexity (caching is harder — no URL-based caching, need normalized caches), requires more server-side work (resolvers, N+1 prevention), and HTTP caching doesn't work out of the box. REST is simpler for CRUD; GraphQL excels for complex, nested data requirements.

cards.question: Explain GraphQL's type system: scalars, objects, enums, unions, interfaces.
cards.answer: Scalars: primitive values — built-in (Int, Float, String, Boolean, ID) and custom (DateTime, JSON, URL). Objects: types with fields, each field has a type: `type User { id: ID! name: String posts: [Post!]! }`. Enums: fixed set of values: `enum Role { ADMIN USER GUEST }`. Input types: used for mutation arguments: `input CreateUserInput { name: String! email: String! }`. Interfaces: abstract types with shared fields that other types implement: `interface Node { id: ID! }`. Unions: a type that could be one of several object types: `union SearchResult = User | Post | Comment`. Non-null (!) and list ([]) modifiers control nullability and cardinality.

cards.question: What are resolvers and how do they work?
cards.answer: Resolvers are functions that produce the data for each field in the schema. Signature: `resolver(parent, args, context, info)`. Parent: the result from the parent resolver (e.g., for User.posts, parent is the User object). Args: field arguments from the query. Context: shared data across all resolvers (database connections, auth info, dataloaders). Info: query metadata (field name, selected fields, AST). Default resolver: if no resolver is defined, GraphQL looks for a property matching the field name on the parent object. Resolver chains: Query.user returns a User, then User.posts resolver fires with that User as parent. This composability is GraphQL's power — each resolver handles one field.

cards.question: What is the N+1 problem in GraphQL and how does DataLoader solve it?
cards.answer: N+1 problem: a query for N users with their posts makes 1 query for users + N queries for each user's posts. With nested data, this explodes exponentially. DataLoader (Facebook) solves this via batching and caching: instead of executing each resolver's database call immediately, DataLoader collects all IDs requested in a single tick, then makes one batch query. Example: `const userLoader = new DataLoader(ids => User.findByIds(ids))`. Each resolver calls `userLoader.load(userId)` — DataLoader batches all load() calls from the same event loop tick into a single findByIds. Per-request caching prevents loading the same ID twice. DataLoader is per-request to avoid stale data.

cards.question: Explain cursor-based vs offset-based pagination in GraphQL.
cards.answer: Offset-based: `query { users(limit: 10, offset: 20) }`. Simple but problems: inconsistent results when data changes between pages (items shift), and performance degrades for large offsets (database must skip N rows). Cursor-based (Relay Connection spec): `query { users(first: 10, after: "cursor") }`. Returns edges (node + cursor) and pageInfo (hasNextPage, endCursor). Cursor is an opaque string (typically encoded ID or timestamp). Advantages: stable pagination (cursor points to a specific item), consistent regardless of insertions/deletions, and efficient (WHERE id > cursor LIMIT 10). The Relay Connection spec is the standard: { edges: [{ node, cursor }], pageInfo: { hasNextPage, endCursor } }.

cards.question: How does Apollo Federation work for GraphQL microservices?
cards.answer: Apollo Federation composes multiple GraphQL services (subgraphs) into a single unified schema via a gateway (Apollo Router). Each subgraph defines its types and can extend types from other subgraphs using @key directive (entity resolution). Example: Users subgraph defines `type User @key(fields: "id") { id: ID! name: String }`. Posts subgraph extends: `extend type User @key(fields: "id") { id: ID! @external posts: [Post!]! }`. The router creates a query plan, fetches User from Users service, then fetches posts from Posts service, and merges results. Federation v2 improvements: @shareable (multiple subgraphs provide a field), @override (migrate fields between subgraphs), and @inaccessible (hide internal fields).

cards.question: How do you handle authentication and authorization in GraphQL?
cards.answer: Authentication (who are you): handled at the HTTP layer before GraphQL — extract JWT/session from Authorization header in middleware, attach user to the context object. Authorization (what can you access): handled in resolvers or directive-based. Approaches: resolver-level checks (`if (!context.user.isAdmin) throw new ForbiddenError()`), schema directives (`@auth(requires: ADMIN)` with a custom directive transformer), or middleware layer (graphql-shield — declarative permission rules). Authorization should be in the business/data layer, not just resolvers (direct database access would bypass resolver auth). Never trust the client — validate permissions server-side even if the UI hides fields.

cards.question: What are GraphQL subscriptions and how are they implemented?
cards.answer: Subscriptions enable real-time, server-pushed updates over a persistent connection. Schema: `type Subscription { messageAdded(channelId: ID!): Message }`. Client subscribes and receives events as they occur. Transport: traditionally WebSocket (graphql-ws protocol), newer approaches use Server-Sent Events (SSE) for simpler infrastructure. Implementation: a PubSub system (in-memory, Redis, Kafka) emits events, subscription resolvers filter and deliver to connected clients. Apollo Server uses `PubSub.publish('MESSAGE_ADDED', { messageAdded })` and resolvers use `subscribe: () => pubsub.asyncIterableIterator(['MESSAGE_ADDED'])`. Scaling requires external PubSub (Redis) since in-memory doesn't work across multiple server instances.

cards.question: How do you cache GraphQL responses?
cards.answer: HTTP caching doesn't work well (POST requests, single endpoint). Client-side: normalized caching (Apollo Client, urql) stores entities by type+ID in a flat store, updates all references when any query returns new data for that entity. Cache policies: cache-first (read cache, fallback to network), network-first (fetch, update cache), cache-and-network (return cache immediately, update with network). Server-side: response caching (hash query + variables, store result), persisted queries (client sends hash instead of full query — CDN-cacheable as GET), and CDN-level caching with cache-control hints per field (@cacheControl directive in Apollo). Automatic cache invalidation is the hardest part — mutations must update or evict affected cache entries.

cards.question: What are fragments and when should you use them?
cards.answer: Fragments are reusable sets of fields on a specific type: `fragment UserFields on User { id name email avatar }`. Use in queries: `query { user(id: 1) { ...UserFields } }`. Benefits: DRY (define common field sets once), co-location (components define their data requirements as fragments), and type safety (fragment is bound to a type). Inline fragments handle union/interface types: `... on Cat { meowVolume } ... on Dog { barkVolume }`. Fragment composition: fragments can include other fragments. Relay and Apollo promote the fragment co-location pattern: each component declares a fragment for its data needs, parent queries compose these fragments. This creates a clear contract between components and their data.

cards.question: How do you handle errors in GraphQL?
cards.answer: GraphQL returns errors in a top-level `errors` array alongside `data` — partial success is possible (some fields resolve, others error). Error format: `{ message, locations, path, extensions }`. Extensions carry custom data (error codes, classification). Strategies: use union types for expected errors (`type CreateUserResult = User | ValidationError | DuplicateEmailError`) — typed, client can handle each case. Use the errors array for unexpected errors (internal server, auth failures). Apollo conventions: `extensions.code` for machine-readable codes (UNAUTHENTICATED, FORBIDDEN, BAD_USER_INPUT). Never expose internal details (stack traces, SQL queries) in production. Error masking: log full error server-side, return sanitized message to client.

cards.question: What is schema stitching and how does it differ from federation?
cards.answer: Schema stitching: combines multiple GraphQL schemas into one by merging types and adding links (type merging, delegation). The gateway fetches types from remote schemas and resolves cross-schema references. Approach: `@merge` directive and type-merging configuration. Federation: purpose-built for microservices. Each subgraph declares its types with `@key`, the router creates an optimized query plan. Key differences: federation has a formal spec (entities, @key, @external), automated query planning, and better tooling. Stitching is more flexible (any schema, no spec required) but manual configuration and less optimized. Recommendation: use Federation for microservices, stitching for combining third-party APIs or legacy schemas.

cards.question: What is introspection in GraphQL and what are the security implications?
cards.answer: Introspection lets clients query the schema itself: `{ __schema { types { name fields { name type { name } } } } }`. Tools like GraphiQL and Apollo Studio use introspection for documentation, autocompletion, and schema exploration. Security concern: introspection exposes your entire API surface — types, fields, arguments, descriptions. Attackers can map your API and find hidden/deprecated fields. Best practice: disable introspection in production (`introspection: false` in Apollo Server), enable only in development. Alternative: use schema registries and persisted queries so clients don't need runtime introspection. Some APIs allow introspection only for authenticated admin users.

cards.question: How do you prevent abuse in GraphQL APIs (rate limiting, query complexity)?
cards.answer: GraphQL's flexibility creates abuse vectors that REST doesn't have. Query complexity analysis: assign cost to each field (scalar = 0, object = 1, list = multiplied by limit argument), reject queries exceeding a threshold. Depth limiting: reject deeply nested queries (prevent `{ user { friends { friends { friends ... } } } }`). graphql-rate-limit: rate limit by IP, user, or field. Persisted queries: whitelist allowed queries — clients send a hash, server only executes known queries. Timeout: abort slow resolvers. Apollo: `ApolloServerPluginLandingPageDisabledDefault()` disables the explorer in production. Maximum batch size for batched queries. These protections are essential — a single GraphQL query can be equivalent to thousands of REST requests.

cards.question: What is GraphQL Code Generation and why use it?
cards.answer: GraphQL Codegen (@graphql-codegen) generates type-safe code from your schema and operations. For TypeScript: generates types for all schema types, typed hooks for each query/mutation (useGetUserQuery returns { data: GetUserQuery }), and typed document nodes. For other languages: generates resolvers (type-safe resolver signatures), SDK clients, and validation. Benefits: no manual type definitions, compile-time errors when queries don't match schema, autocomplete in IDE, and automatic updates when schema changes. Configuration: codegen.yml specifies schema source, documents (query files), and plugins. Watch mode regenerates on file changes. Integrates with Apollo, urql, and React Query.

cards.question: How do GraphQL directives work?
cards.answer: Directives annotate schema elements or query operations with extra behavior. Built-in: @skip(if: Boolean) and @include(if: Boolean) for conditional fields in queries, @deprecated(reason: String) for schema documentation. Custom directives: defined in schema (`directive @auth(requires: Role) on FIELD_DEFINITION`) and implemented as schema transformers. Examples: @cacheControl (set caching hints per field), @auth (authorization checks), @rateLimit (per-field rate limiting), @upper (transform string to uppercase). In Apollo Server: implemented via mapSchema with visitor pattern. Custom directives are powerful for cross-cutting concerns but can make the schema harder to understand — use judiciously.

cards.question: What is the Relay specification and why does it matter?
cards.answer: Relay is Facebook's GraphQL client with specific schema conventions: Global Object Identification (every entity has a globally unique ID, queryable via `node(id: ID!)` — enables normalized caching), Connections (cursor-based pagination with edges, nodes, and pageInfo), and Input Object Mutations (mutations take a single input argument and return a clientMutationId). Even if you don't use Relay as a client, adopting its conventions is considered best practice because they solve real problems: global IDs enable cache normalization across types, connections standardize pagination, and the node interface enables refetching any entity. Apollo Client works well with Relay-style schemas.

cards.question: How do you design a good GraphQL schema?
cards.answer: Principles: design for the client use case (not the database schema), use descriptive names (createUser not addUser if it's a creation), nullable by default (non-null only when you can guarantee it), use enums over strings for fixed values, prefer specific mutation return types over generic success/failure, and use input types for mutation arguments. Avoid: exposing database IDs directly (use opaque global IDs), deep nesting without pagination (always paginate lists), generic types (type Response { success: Boolean data: JSON }), and breaking changes to published schemas. Versioning: GraphQL discourages URL versioning — use @deprecated and additive changes. Schema-first vs code-first: both work, choose based on team workflow.

cards.question: What are persisted queries and how do they improve performance?
cards.answer: Persisted queries replace full query strings with short hashes. Client sends `{ id: "abc123", variables: {...} }` instead of the full query text. Benefits: smaller request payload (query strings can be huge), CDN caching (GET requests with query hash as key), and security (server only executes whitelisted queries — prevents arbitrary queries). Implementation: at build time, extract all queries and generate a mapping (hash → query string). Register with the server. Apollo's Automatic Persisted Queries (APQ): client sends hash first, if server doesn't recognize it, client resends with full query, server caches for future. Trusted documents take this further — only pre-registered queries are allowed, completely preventing ad-hoc queries in production.

cards.question: How do mutations work in GraphQL?
cards.answer: Mutations are GraphQL's write operations. Unlike queries (which execute in parallel), mutation fields execute sequentially (guaranteed order). Schema: `type Mutation { createUser(input: CreateUserInput!): CreateUserPayload! }`. Input types separate write arguments from read types. Return the created/modified entity so the client cache can update: `type CreateUserPayload { user: User! }`. For error handling: union return types (`CreateUserPayload | ValidationError`) or the errors array. Optimistic updates: Apollo Client can predict the mutation result and update the UI immediately, rolling back if the server disagrees. Always return enough data in the mutation response for the client to update its cache without a refetch.

cards.question: What is nullability design in GraphQL schemas?
cards.answer: In GraphQL, fields are nullable by default — `name: String` can return null. Non-null (`name: String!`) guarantees a value. Design considerations: non-null is convenient for clients (no null checks) but dangerous for servers — if a non-null field resolver throws, the error bubbles up to the nearest nullable parent, potentially nulling out a larger response. Best practices: make fields non-null when the data is always available (id: ID!), keep fields nullable when they depend on external services or complex joins (if the service is down, return null for that field instead of failing the entire query). Lists: `[User!]!` means the list is non-null and contains non-null users. Over-using non-null reduces GraphQL's partial-success advantage.

cards.question: How do you test GraphQL APIs?
cards.answer: Unit testing resolvers: test resolver functions directly with mocked context and parent objects. No need for the full GraphQL server. Integration testing: use test server (Apollo's executeOperation or supertest) to send full queries and assert responses. Schema testing: validate schema is valid, check for breaking changes with graphql-inspector or Apollo's schema checks. Client testing: mock the GraphQL layer in component tests (MockedProvider in Apollo, MSW for intercepting network). Contract testing: ensure client queries match the server schema (codegen catches mismatches). Load testing: k6 or Artillery with GraphQL queries of varying complexity. Test error cases: invalid inputs, auth failures, partial errors. Snapshot testing schema SDL detects unintended changes.

cards.question: What are the differences between EC2 On-Demand, Reserved, and Spot instances?
cards.answer: On-Demand instances charge per second with no commitment — best for unpredictable workloads. Reserved Instances (1 or 3 year term) offer up to 72% discount for steady-state usage; you can choose Standard (fixed instance type) or Convertible (can change family/OS). Spot Instances let you bid on unused capacity at up to 90% discount, but AWS can reclaim them with a 2-minute warning — ideal for fault-tolerant batch jobs, CI/CD, or HPC. There are also Savings Plans, which commit to a $/hour spend rather than a specific instance type.

cards.question: How does AWS Lambda handle concurrency, and what is provisioned concurrency?
cards.answer: Lambda scales automatically by running a separate instance of your function for each concurrent request. The default regional concurrency limit is 1,000 (adjustable). When a new instance is needed, Lambda performs a cold start — downloading code, initializing the runtime, and running your init code. Provisioned concurrency pre-warms a specified number of execution environments so they are always ready, eliminating cold starts. You pay for provisioned concurrency even when idle. Reserved concurrency caps a function's max concurrency to prevent it from consuming the entire account quota. Use provisioned concurrency for latency-sensitive APIs and reserved concurrency for throttling or isolation.

cards.question: What is a VPC, and what are the key networking components you configure inside one?
cards.answer: A VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) is an isolated virtual network within AWS where you launch resources. Key components: Subnets — segment your VPC by AZ; public subnets have a route to an Internet Gateway, private subnets do not. Internet Gateway (IGW) — allows internet-bound traffic from public subnets. NAT Gateway — lets private subnet resources reach the internet without being publicly accessible. Route Tables — control traffic routing per subnet. Security Groups — stateful firewalls at the instance level (allow rules only). Network ACLs — stateless firewalls at the subnet level (allow and deny rules). VPC Peering or Transit Gateway connects VPCs together. VPC Endpoints provide private connectivity to AWS services like S3 and DynamoDB without traversing the internet.

cards.question: Explain the S3 storage classes and when you would use each.
cards.answer: S3 Standard — low latency, high throughput for frequently accessed data. S3 Intelligent-Tiering — automatically moves objects between frequent and infrequent tiers based on access patterns (small monthly monitoring fee). S3 Standard-IA (Infrequent Access) — lower storage cost, higher retrieval cost; 30-day minimum storage, 128KB minimum size. S3 One Zone-IA — same as Standard-IA but in a single AZ; cheaper, less durable against AZ failure. S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval — archive with millisecond retrieval, 90-day minimum. S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval — minutes to hours retrieval (Expedited: 1-5 min, Standard: 3-5 hr, Bulk: 5-12 hr), 90-day minimum. S3 Glacier Deep Archive — lowest cost, 12-48 hour retrieval, 180-day minimum. Use Lifecycle policies to automatically transition objects between classes based on age.

cards.question: How does IAM policy evaluation work when multiple policies apply?
cards.answer: IAM uses a deny-by-default model with this evaluation order: 1) Explicit Deny — if any policy explicitly denies the action, it is denied (deny always wins). 2) Service Control Policies (SCPs) — if the account is in an AWS Organization, the SCP must allow the action. 3) Resource-based policies — policies on the resource (e.g., S3 bucket policy) can grant cross-account access. 4) Identity-based policies — policies attached to the IAM user/role/group. 5) Permissions boundaries — if set, the effective permissions are the intersection of the identity policy and the boundary. 6) Session policies — for temporary credentials, further constrain the session. The final result is ALLOW only if at least one policy allows the action AND no policy denies it, AND all applicable boundaries/SCPs also allow it.

cards.question: What is the difference between an Application Load Balancer (ALB) and a Network Load Balancer (NLB)?
cards.answer: ALB operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) and supports path-based routing, host-based routing, HTTP header/method routing, and WebSocket. It can terminate TLS, integrate with WAF, and route to targets by content. Best for microservices and containerized apps. NLB operates at Layer 4 (TCP/UDP/TLS) and is designed for ultra-high performance — handles millions of requests per second with ultra-low latency. NLB preserves the client source IP, supports static IPs and Elastic IPs per AZ, and can handle volatile traffic patterns. Use NLB for TCP-based protocols (gaming, IoT, non-HTTP), and ALB when you need content-based routing or HTTP-specific features. Both support cross-zone load balancing and health checks.

cards.question: How would you design a highly available, multi-AZ architecture on AWS?
cards.answer: Spread resources across at least 2 AZs (ideally 3) within a region. Use an ALB or NLB to distribute traffic across AZs. For compute: deploy EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling Group spanning multiple AZs, or use ECS/EKS with tasks in multiple AZs. For databases: use RDS Multi-AZ (synchronous standby replica with automatic failover) or Aurora (multi-AZ by design, up to 15 read replicas). For storage: S3 is automatically replicated across 3+ AZs. Use ElastiCache with Multi-AZ and automatic failover. Route53 health checks route DNS away from unhealthy regions. Decouple components with SQS/SNS. Use NAT Gateways in each AZ to avoid cross-AZ single points of failure. For disaster recovery across regions, consider cross-region replication for S3, Aurora Global Database, and Route53 failover routing.

cards.question: What is DynamoDB and how does its partition key design affect performance?
cards.answer: DynamoDB is a fully managed NoSQL key-value and document database that delivers single-digit millisecond performance at any scale. Tables are partitioned by a partition key (hash key); optionally with a sort key for range queries. Poor partition key design (e.g., using a status field with only 3 values) creates hot partitions — all reads/writes funnel to a few partitions, causing throttling even when total table capacity is not exhausted. Good partition keys have high cardinality and uniform access patterns (e.g., userId, orderId). If you must use a low-cardinality key, add a random suffix (write sharding) or use a composite key. DynamoDB also supports on-demand capacity (pay-per-request) and provisioned capacity with auto-scaling. Global Secondary Indexes (GSIs) let you query on non-key attributes but consume their own throughput.

cards.question: Explain CloudFormation and how it differs from the AWS CDK.
cards.answer: CloudFormation is AWS's IaC service — you define infrastructure in JSON or YAML templates, and CloudFormation provisions and manages resources as a stack. It handles dependency ordering, rollback on failure, drift detection, and change sets (preview changes before applying). The AWS CDK (Cloud Development Kit) lets you define infrastructure using familiar programming languages (TypeScript, Python, Java, Go, C#). CDK code synthesizes into CloudFormation templates. CDK advantages: real programming constructs (loops, conditionals, abstractions), higher-level 'L2' constructs that provide sensible defaults (e.g., `new s3.Bucket()` automatically sets encryption), and type safety. CloudFormation advantages: no build step, widely understood, direct template control. Use `cdk diff` to preview changes, `cdk deploy` to apply. CDK is generally preferred for new projects; raw CloudFormation for teams that need maximum template-level control or when CDK constructs don't exist.

cards.question: What is Amazon ECS and how does it compare to EKS?
cards.answer: ECS (Elastic Container Service) is AWS's proprietary container orchestrator. You define Tasks (one or more containers) and Services (desired count, load balancer, scaling). ECS can run on EC2 instances you manage or on AWS Fargate (serverless — no servers to manage). EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) runs upstream Kubernetes on AWS. EKS is ideal if you need Kubernetes portability, its ecosystem (Helm, Istio, ArgoCD), or run multi-cloud. ECS is simpler to operate with deeper AWS integration (IAM task roles, CloudMap service discovery, App Mesh). ECS has no control plane cost; EKS charges $0.10/hr per cluster for the managed control plane. Choose ECS for AWS-native, simpler workloads; EKS when you need Kubernetes-specific features, multi-cloud strategy, or your team already knows Kubernetes.

cards.question: What is Route53 and what routing policies does it support?
cards.answer: Route53 is AWS's managed DNS service and domain registrar. It supports several routing policies: Simple — returns a single resource (or multiple values, chosen randomly by the client). Weighted — distribute traffic by percentage across resources (e.g., 70/30 for blue-green). Latency-based — routes to the AWS region with the lowest latency for the user. Failover — active/passive setup with health checks; routes to secondary when primary fails. Geolocation — routes based on the user's geographic location (continent, country, or US state). Geoproximity — routes based on geographic distance, with an adjustable bias to shift traffic. Multivalue answer — returns up to 8 healthy records (basic DNS-level load balancing with health checks). Route53 also supports alias records for AWS resources (free queries, unlike CNAMEs), DNSSEC signing, and private hosted zones within VPCs.

cards.question: How do you secure data at rest and in transit on AWS?
cards.answer: At rest: Use AWS KMS (Key Management Service) to create and manage encryption keys. S3 supports SSE-S3 (AWS-managed keys), SSE-KMS (KMS-managed with audit trail in CloudTrail), and SSE-C (customer-provided keys). EBS volumes support KMS encryption (enable default encryption for the account). RDS supports encryption at creation time using KMS. DynamoDB supports encryption by default with AWS-owned, AWS-managed, or customer-managed KMS keys. In transit: Enforce TLS everywhere. Use ACM (AWS Certificate Manager) for free public TLS certificates on ALB, CloudFront, and API Gateway. Set S3 bucket policies to deny non-HTTPS requests (`aws:SecureTransport` condition). Use VPC endpoints for private connectivity to AWS services. For cross-region or VPN traffic, use AWS Site-to-Site VPN or AWS Direct Connect with MACsec encryption. Enable CloudTrail to audit all KMS key usage.

cards.question: What happens when an EC2 instance in an Auto Scaling Group fails a health check?
cards.answer: The Auto Scaling Group (ASG) detects the unhealthy instance via EC2 status checks or ELB health checks (if configured). The ASG terminates the unhealthy instance and launches a replacement to maintain the desired capacity. The sequence: 1) Instance marked unhealthy. 2) ASG sets instance to Terminating state. 3) Lifecycle hooks fire (if configured) — you can run cleanup scripts or drain connections. 4) Instance is terminated. 5) ASG launches a new instance in the AZ to rebalance. The health check grace period (default 300s) prevents premature termination of instances still starting up. If using ELB health checks, the instance must pass both EC2 status checks AND ELB health checks. You can configure a warm pool to keep pre-initialized stopped instances ready for faster replacement.

cards.question: Design a serverless REST API on AWS. What services would you use?
cards.answer: API Gateway (REST or HTTP API) receives requests and routes them. Lambda functions handle business logic for each endpoint. DynamoDB or Aurora Serverless v2 for the database. Cognito for authentication — JWT tokens validated at the API Gateway authorizer level. For the pipeline: API Gateway validates the request, invokes the Lambda, Lambda queries/writes to the database, and returns the response. Add CloudFront in front of API Gateway for edge caching. Use SQS for async processing (e.g., sending emails). Step Functions for complex workflows. S3 for file uploads (presigned URLs from Lambda). CloudWatch for logging and metrics, X-Ray for distributed tracing. IAM roles grant each Lambda only the permissions it needs (least privilege). Use SAM or CDK to define the entire stack as IaC. Cost is purely pay-per-use: API Gateway per-request pricing + Lambda per-invocation + DynamoDB per-read/write.

cards.question: What is CloudWatch and what monitoring capabilities does it provide?
cards.answer: CloudWatch is AWS's monitoring and observability service. Core capabilities: Metrics — collect and track standard metrics (CPU, network, disk for EC2; invocations, errors, duration for Lambda; read/write capacity for DynamoDB). Custom metrics via PutMetricData API. Alarms — trigger actions (SNS notification, Auto Scaling policy, EC2 action) when a metric crosses a threshold. Supports static thresholds and anomaly detection. Logs — centralized log collection via the CloudWatch agent or direct SDK logging. Log Insights lets you query logs with a SQL-like syntax. Dashboards — visualize metrics and logs in real-time. Events/EventBridge — react to state changes in AWS resources (EC2 state change, CodePipeline failure) and route them to targets (Lambda, SQS, Step Functions). Contributor Insights identifies top-N contributors to a metric. Synthetics creates canary scripts that monitor endpoints. ServiceLens integrates with X-Ray for end-to-end tracing.

cards.question: How does AWS X-Ray help with debugging distributed applications?
cards.answer: X-Ray collects trace data as requests flow through your distributed application. You instrument your code with the X-Ray SDK (or enable it automatically for Lambda, API Gateway, and App Mesh). Each request gets a unique trace ID. X-Ray creates a Service Map — a visual graph showing your application's components and the connections between them, with latency and error rates on each edge. Traces show the full request path with timing for each segment (e.g., API Gateway -> Lambda -> DynamoDB -> S3). Subsegments break down individual service calls. You can filter traces by response time, status code, annotation, or metadata. Sampling rules control what percentage of requests are traced (to manage cost). X-Ray integrates with CloudWatch ServiceLens for a unified view. Use X-Ray to identify bottlenecks, find which downstream service is causing latency, and debug 5xx errors across microservices.

cards.question: Compare SQS and SNS. When would you use each?
cards.answer: SQS (Simple Queue Service) is a fully managed message queue. Messages are pulled by consumers, stored until processed (up to 14 days), and processed by exactly one consumer (Standard queue) or exactly once (FIFO queue). Standard queues offer nearly unlimited throughput with at-least-once delivery; FIFO queues guarantee ordering and exactly-once processing at up to 3,000 messages/sec (with batching). SNS (Simple Notification Service) is a pub/sub service. Messages are pushed to multiple subscribers simultaneously — Lambda, SQS, HTTP endpoints, email, SMS. Use SQS when you need decoupled processing, backpressure handling, and guaranteed delivery to a single consumer. Use SNS when you need fan-out to multiple consumers. The common pattern is SNS + SQS fan-out: SNS pushes to multiple SQS queues, each processed independently. This combines fan-out with reliable per-consumer delivery.

cards.question: What are the pillars of the AWS Well-Architected Framework?
cards.answer: The six pillars are: 1) Operational Excellence — automate changes, respond to events, define standards for daily operations (IaC, CI/CD, runbooks, observability). 2) Security — protect data, systems, and assets. Implement identity foundation (least privilege), enable traceability, automate security best practices, protect data in transit and at rest. 3) Reliability — ability to recover from failures. Design for failure: multi-AZ, auto-scaling, backup/restore, chaos engineering, bulkhead patterns. 4) Performance Efficiency — use computing resources efficiently. Select the right resource types, monitor performance, use managed services, experiment. 5) Cost Optimization — avoid unnecessary costs. Right-size instances, use Savings Plans/Reserved Instances, terminate unused resources, use spot where possible. 6) Sustainability — minimize environmental impact of cloud workloads. AWS provides the Well-Architected Tool to review workloads against these pillars and generate improvement plans.

cards.question: How would you implement cross-account access in AWS?
cards.answer: The preferred method is IAM role assumption (AssumeRole). In the target account, create an IAM role with a trust policy that allows the source account's principal (user, role, or root). The trust policy specifies the source account ID and optionally an ExternalId for third-party access. In the source account, the IAM user/role needs a policy with `sts:AssumeRole` permission for the target role's ARN. The workflow: source calls `sts:AssumeRole`, receives temporary credentials, and uses them to act in the target account. For organizations, use AWS Organizations SCPs to set permission guardrails. Resource-based policies (S3 bucket policies, KMS key policies, Lambda resource policies) can also grant cross-account access directly without role assumption. For cross-account networking, use VPC Peering, Transit Gateway, or PrivateLink. AWS RAM (Resource Access Manager) shares resources like subnets, Transit Gateways, and Route53 rules across accounts.

cards.question: What is Amazon RDS Multi-AZ and how does failover work?
cards.answer: RDS Multi-AZ creates a synchronous standby replica in a different Availability Zone. The primary handles all reads and writes; the standby receives synchronous block-level replication. On failure — hardware fault, AZ outage, or manual failover — RDS automatically flips the DNS CNAME to the standby, which becomes the new primary. Failover typically completes in 60-120 seconds. Applications using the RDS endpoint don't need code changes since the DNS resolves to the new primary. Multi-AZ is for high availability, not read scaling — the standby does not serve read traffic (use Read Replicas for that). For Aurora, Multi-AZ works differently: Aurora has a shared storage layer replicated 6 ways across 3 AZs. Aurora replicas can serve reads AND be promoted to primary on failover (typically under 30 seconds). Aurora also supports Multi-Master for write availability across AZs.

cards.question: What is AWS Step Functions and when would you use it over direct Lambda orchestration?
cards.answer: Step Functions is a serverless workflow orchestrator that coordinates multiple AWS services using a JSON-based state machine definition (Amazon States Language). It provides built-in retry with exponential backoff, catch/fallback states, parallel execution, map states for dynamic parallelism, wait states, and choice states for branching logic. Use Step Functions over direct Lambda-to-Lambda calls when: workflows have complex branching or conditional logic, you need visual tracking of execution state, retries and error handling must be reliable and auditable, workflows are long-running (up to 1 year with Standard workflows), or you need human approval steps. Standard Workflows support exactly-once execution for up to a year. Express Workflows handle high-volume event processing (up to 5 minutes, at-least-once). Direct Lambda invocation (one Lambda calling another) is simpler but creates tight coupling, makes error handling your responsibility, and provides no execution visibility.

cards.question: How does Amazon CloudFront work and what are its key features?
cards.answer: CloudFront is AWS's global CDN with 450+ edge locations. It caches content close to users, reducing latency. Key features: Origin types — S3 buckets (with Origin Access Control for private buckets), ALB, EC2, API Gateway, or any HTTP server. Behaviors — route requests by URL path pattern to different origins with different caching rules. Cache policies control TTLs and what's included in the cache key (headers, query strings, cookies). Origin request policies control what's forwarded to the origin. Lambda@Edge runs code at edge locations on viewer request/response and origin request/response (e.g., A/B testing, URL rewrites, auth). CloudFront Functions run lightweight JavaScript at the edge for header manipulation and URL rewrites at 1/6th the cost of Lambda@Edge. SSL/TLS with ACM certificates, HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 support, real-time logs, and AWS Shield Standard (DDoS protection) included at no extra cost.

cards.question: What is the Shared Responsibility Model in AWS?
cards.answer: AWS and the customer share security responsibilities. AWS is responsible for security OF the cloud: physical data center security, hardware, networking infrastructure, hypervisor, and managed service internals (e.g., patching RDS database engines, managing S3 storage hardware). The customer is responsible for security IN the cloud: guest OS patching and configuration (EC2), network configuration (security groups, NACLs), IAM (users, roles, policies, MFA), application code, data encryption (choosing to enable it), and firewall rules. The boundary shifts with the service model: for IaaS (EC2), the customer manages more; for PaaS (RDS), AWS handles OS patching; for SaaS/Serverless (Lambda, S3), AWS manages almost everything, and the customer focuses on IAM, data, and encryption configuration. Understanding this model is critical for compliance — you must know which controls you own vs. which AWS owns.

cards.question: Design a CI/CD pipeline on AWS for deploying a containerized microservice.
cards.answer: Source: CodeCommit (or GitHub with a CodeStar connection) triggers the pipeline on push. Build: CodeBuild runs unit tests, builds the Docker image, pushes it to ECR (Elastic Container Registry), and scans the image for vulnerabilities with ECR image scanning. Deploy: CodeDeploy or ECS rolling/blue-green deployment updates the ECS service. Full flow with CodePipeline: Source stage (CodeCommit) -> Build stage (CodeBuild: test, build, push to ECR) -> Deploy stage (ECS blue-green via CodeDeploy: deploys new task definition, shifts ALB target group traffic, rolls back on CloudWatch alarm). Add a manual approval stage before production. Use Parameter Store or Secrets Manager for config/secrets injected at deploy time. CloudWatch dashboards and alarms monitor deployment health. For advanced setups, use CDK Pipelines (self-mutating pipeline defined in CDK) or GitOps with EKS and ArgoCD. Alternative: GitHub Actions with OIDC federation to assume an IAM role — no long-lived AWS credentials needed.

cards.question: What is AWS Organizations and how do you structure a multi-account setup?
cards.answer: AWS Organizations lets you centrally manage multiple AWS accounts. You create an Organization from a management account, then organize member accounts into Organizational Units (OUs). Service Control Policies (SCPs) attached to OUs set permission guardrails — they restrict what actions accounts in the OU can perform (even root users). Recommended OU structure: Security OU (log archive account with CloudTrail/Config aggregation, security tooling account with GuardDuty/Security Hub), Infrastructure OU (shared networking with Transit Gateway, DNS), Workloads OU (separate accounts per environment or team — dev, staging, prod), Sandbox OU (experimentation with strict budget SCPs). Enable AWS Control Tower to automate account provisioning with guardrails. Use consolidated billing for volume discounts and a single payment. AWS SSO (IAM Identity Center) provides single sign-on across all accounts with permission sets mapped to IAM roles. This multi-account strategy provides blast radius isolation, clear billing, and least-privilege boundaries.

cards.question: What are Azure Availability Zones and how do they differ from Availability Sets?
cards.answer: Availability Zones (AZs) are physically separate data centers within an Azure region, each with independent power, cooling, and networking. Deploying across AZs protects against entire data center failures. Azure guarantees 99.99% uptime SLA for VMs across 2+ AZs. Availability Sets are a legacy HA mechanism within a single data center — they distribute VMs across Fault Domains (separate racks/power) and Update Domains (staggered maintenance groups). Availability Sets provide 99.95% SLA. Key differences: AZs protect against data center failure; Availability Sets only protect against rack/maintenance failure. AZs have slightly higher network latency between zones (sub-2ms). For new deployments, prefer AZs. Use Zone-Redundant services when available (e.g., Zone-Redundant Storage, Azure SQL with zone redundancy, Standard Load Balancer which is zone-redundant by default). Availability Sets are still useful in regions that don't support AZs.

cards.question: Explain Azure Functions and its hosting plans.
cards.answer: Azure Functions is Azure's serverless compute service for event-driven code. Three hosting plans: Consumption Plan — true serverless; scales to zero, scales out automatically, pay only for execution time and memory. Cold starts can occur. Max 5-minute execution (configurable to 10). Functions Premium Plan — pre-warmed instances eliminate cold starts, VNet integration, unlimited execution duration, more powerful instance sizes. You pay for a minimum number of always-ready instances plus any burst instances. Dedicated (App Service) Plan — run functions on existing App Service infrastructure; good when you already have underutilized App Service plans, need predictable billing, or manual scale control. Triggers include HTTP, Timer (CRON), Blob Storage, Queue Storage, Service Bus, Event Hub, Cosmos DB change feed, Event Grid, and more. Bindings provide declarative input/output connections to services. Durable Functions extend Azure Functions with stateful orchestration patterns (chaining, fan-out/fan-in, human interaction, monitors).

cards.question: What is Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) and how do you manage it?
cards.answer: AKS is Azure's managed Kubernetes service. Azure manages the control plane (API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager) at no charge — you only pay for worker node VMs. Key features: Node pools — multiple pools with different VM sizes (e.g., GPU nodes for ML, spot nodes for batch). Azure CNI networking — pods get VNet IPs directly (or use kubenet for overlay). Azure AD integration — use Entra ID for RBAC authentication, map Azure AD groups to Kubernetes ClusterRoleBindings. Managed identity — the cluster uses a managed identity for Azure resource access (ACR pull, disk creation). Azure Monitor for Containers — collects node and pod metrics, container logs to Log Analytics workspace. Cluster autoscaler scales node count; KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) scales pods based on external metrics. AKS supports Workload Identity (federated tokens for pod-level Azure access), Azure Policy for Kubernetes (Gatekeeper/OPA), and gitops with Flux v2.

cards.question: How does Azure Virtual Network (VNet) work and what are its key components?
cards.answer: A VNet is an isolated network in Azure where you deploy resources. Key components: Subnets — segment the VNet; resources in a subnet can communicate by default. Network Security Groups (NSGs) — stateful firewalls with allow/deny rules based on source/destination IP, port, and protocol. Applied to subnets or individual NICs. NSG rules have priorities (lower number = higher priority). Service Endpoints — provide direct, optimized connectivity to Azure services (Storage, SQL) over the Azure backbone. Private Endpoints — assign a private IP from your VNet to an Azure service (e.g., Storage Account), accessible only within the VNet. VNet Peering — connect VNets with low-latency, high-bandwidth links (global peering works cross-region). Azure Virtual WAN — hub-and-spoke topology at scale. Azure Firewall — managed, stateful firewall with FQDN filtering, threat intelligence, and centralized logging. User-Defined Routes (UDRs) — override Azure's default routing to force traffic through a firewall or NVA.

cards.question: What is Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD) and how does it differ from on-prem Active Directory?
cards.answer: Entra ID is Azure's cloud identity and access management service. It handles authentication (OAuth 2.0, OIDC, SAML), user/group management, conditional access policies, and application registration. Key differences from on-prem AD: Entra ID uses flat structure (no OUs, forests, or domains) — instead uses groups and administrative units. It communicates over HTTPS (REST APIs), not LDAP/Kerberos. No Group Policy Objects — use Intune for device management. Entra ID supports: Conditional Access — enforce MFA, device compliance, location-based restrictions per app. App registrations — OAuth/OIDC for SPA, web, mobile, and daemon apps. Service Principals and Managed Identities for application-to-Azure authentication. B2B (guest users from other tenants) and B2C (customer-facing identity). Hybrid: Azure AD Connect syncs on-prem AD users/groups to Entra ID. Entra ID P1/P2 tiers add conditional access, PIM (Privileged Identity Management for just-in-time admin access), and identity protection (risk-based sign-in policies).

cards.question: Explain Azure RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) and how it works with scopes.
cards.answer: Azure RBAC controls who can do what on Azure resources. A role assignment has three elements: Security Principal (who — user, group, service principal, or managed identity), Role Definition (what — a collection of allowed actions like `Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action`), and Scope (where — management group, subscription, resource group, or individual resource). Roles are hierarchical: assignments at a higher scope are inherited by child scopes. Built-in roles include Owner (full access + assign roles), Contributor (full access, cannot assign roles), Reader (view only), and 400+ service-specific roles (e.g., Storage Blob Data Contributor, AKS Cluster Admin). Custom roles let you define exactly which actions/dataActions to allow or deny. Best practices: assign roles to groups (not individual users), use least privilege, prefer built-in roles, use Entra PIM for just-in-time elevation of privileged roles. Deny assignments (from Azure Blueprints) override allows. Check effective access with `az role assignment list` or the IAM blade.

cards.question: What is Azure Cosmos DB and how do you choose the right consistency level?
cards.answer: Cosmos DB is a globally distributed, multi-model NoSQL database with single-digit millisecond latency. It supports document (Core SQL API), key-value (Table API), column-family (Cassandra API), graph (Gremlin API), and MongoDB wire protocol. Five consistency levels (strongest to weakest): Strong — linearizable reads, global order guaranteed (only available in single-region writes). Bounded Staleness — reads lag behind writes by at most K operations or T seconds; strong within a region. Session — reads within a session see their own writes (most popular, default). Consistent Prefix — reads never see out-of-order writes, but may be stale. Eventual — no ordering guarantee, lowest latency. Choose based on your tradeoff: Strong for financial data (highest latency/cost, lowest throughput). Session for most applications (user always sees their own writes). Eventual for telemetry/analytics where staleness is acceptable. Cosmos DB uses Request Units (RU/s) for throughput provisioning. Partitioning strategy (partition key) is critical — same principles as DynamoDB.

cards.question: What is Azure Blob Storage and what are the access tiers?
cards.answer: Azure Blob Storage is object storage for unstructured data. Three blob types: Block blobs (files up to 190.7 TiB, optimized for upload/download), Append blobs (optimized for append operations like logs), Page blobs (random read/write, used for VM disks). Access tiers: Hot — frequently accessed data, highest storage cost, lowest access cost. Cool — infrequently accessed, lower storage cost, higher access cost, 30-day minimum storage. Cold — rarely accessed data, even lower storage cost, higher access cost, 90-day minimum storage. Archive — offline storage, lowest cost, hours to rehydrate (Standard: 15 hours, High Priority: 1 hour), 180-day minimum. Set the default tier at the storage account level; override per blob. Lifecycle management policies automate tier transitions (e.g., move to Cool after 30 days, Archive after 90). Immutable storage (WORM) prevents deletion/modification for compliance. Soft delete protects against accidental deletion. Versioning keeps previous blob versions.

cards.question: How would you set up a CI/CD pipeline using Azure DevOps?
cards.answer: Azure DevOps provides Azure Repos (Git), Azure Pipelines (CI/CD), Azure Boards (work tracking), Azure Artifacts (package feeds), and Azure Test Plans. A typical CI/CD pipeline: CI Pipeline (YAML in `azure-pipelines.yml`): Trigger on PR or push to main. Stages: 1) Build — install dependencies, compile, run unit tests. 2) Docker Build — build container image, push to Azure Container Registry (ACR). 3) Security Scan — run Trivy or Microsoft Defender for container scanning. CD Pipeline: 1) Deploy to Dev — auto-deploy on merge to main using Environments with deployment jobs. 2) Deploy to Staging — use approval gates (manual or automated health checks). 3) Deploy to Production — canary or blue-green deployment with Azure App Service deployment slots or AKS rolling updates. Key features: Service Connections for secure Azure authentication (service principal or managed identity). Variable Groups and Azure Key Vault integration for secrets. Multi-stage YAML pipelines checked into source control. Environments with checks (approvals, business hours, branch control). Self-hosted agents for private network access.

cards.question: What is Azure Front Door and how does it compare to Azure Application Gateway?
cards.answer: Azure Front Door is a global Layer 7 load balancer and CDN with built-in WAF. It operates at Microsoft's edge network (200+ global PoPs). Features: global HTTP load balancing with latency-based routing, SSL offload, URL path-based routing, session affinity, caching, and Web Application Firewall (WAF) policies. It supports health probes and automatic failover across backend pools (can span regions). Azure Application Gateway is a regional Layer 7 load balancer deployed within a VNet. Features: URL path-based routing, multi-site hosting, SSL termination, WAF, autoscaling (v2), private frontend IP for internal load balancing. Use Front Door for global applications spanning multiple regions — it routes users to the closest healthy backend. Use Application Gateway for regional applications that need advanced routing within a single region or private network. Common pattern: Front Door globally in front of Application Gateways in each region, which then route to backend services (AKS, App Service, VMs).

cards.question: Explain Azure Resource Manager (ARM) templates and how Bicep improves on them.
cards.answer: ARM templates are JSON files that declaratively define Azure infrastructure. They specify resources, parameters, variables, outputs, and dependencies. ARM handles deployment orchestration, dependency ordering, and idempotent deployments. Challenges with ARM JSON: verbose syntax, no comments (without workarounds), complex expressions using nested function calls (e.g., `[concat(resourceGroup().location)]`), difficult to modularize. Bicep is a domain-specific language that transpiles to ARM JSON. Improvements: Clean, concise syntax (roughly 50% fewer lines). First-class modules for reusability. Direct resource reference syntax (`storageAccount.properties.primaryEndpoints`). Type safety and IntelliSense in VS Code. No state file — Bicep/ARM uses Azure itself as the source of truth (unlike Terraform). Example: `resource sa 'Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts@2023-01-01' = { name: storageName, location: location, sku: { name: 'Standard_LRS' }, kind: 'StorageV2' }`. Deploy with `az deployment group create --template-file main.bicep`. Bicep is the recommended IaC tool for Azure-only environments. For multi-cloud, Terraform is preferred.

cards.question: What is Azure Monitor and how does it integrate with Application Insights?
cards.answer: Azure Monitor is the unified monitoring platform for all Azure resources. It collects: Metrics — numeric time-series data (CPU, memory, request count) stored in a time-series database with 1-minute granularity. Logs — structured log data stored in Log Analytics workspaces, queried with Kusto Query Language (KQL). Activity Logs — subscription-level events (resource creation, role assignments). Application Insights is an Azure Monitor feature for application performance monitoring (APM). It provides: distributed tracing across services, dependency tracking (SQL, HTTP calls), exception logging with stack traces, live metrics stream, availability tests (ping and multi-step), smart detection (anomaly alerts), and application map (visual dependency graph). Auto-instrumentation is available for .NET, Java, Node.js, and Python — often requires zero code changes. Custom telemetry via the SDK: `TelemetryClient.TrackEvent()`, `TrackMetric()`, `TrackDependency()`. Alerts can trigger action groups (email, SMS, webhook, Logic App, Azure Function, ITSM connector). Workbooks combine metrics, logs, and text into interactive reports.

cards.question: How does Azure SQL Database differ from SQL Server on Azure VMs?
cards.answer: Azure SQL Database is a fully managed PaaS — Microsoft handles patching, backups, high availability, and scaling. You choose a service tier: General Purpose (standard workloads, remote storage), Business Critical (low latency, local SSD, built-in read replica), or Hyperscale (up to 100TB, rapid scale-out, near-instant backups via page server architecture). Purchasing models: DTU (bundled CPU/IO/memory units) or vCore (choose CPU/memory independently). SQL Server on Azure VMs is IaaS — you manage the OS, patching, backup, HA configuration (Always On AG). You get full SQL Server feature parity (CLR, SSIS, SQL Agent jobs). Choose Azure SQL DB when: you want zero administration, elastic scaling, built-in HA (99.99% SLA), and can work within PaaS constraints (no OS access, some feature gaps). Choose SQL on VM when: you need full feature parity, OS access, or are doing lift-and-shift of a complex existing SQL Server. Azure SQL Managed Instance is a middle ground — nearly full SQL Server compatibility with PaaS management, VNet-native deployment, and SQL Agent support.

cards.question: Design a multi-region, highly available web application on Azure.
cards.answer: Global entry point: Azure Front Door routes users to the nearest healthy region using latency-based routing with health probes. Per-region architecture: Azure Application Gateway (WAF-enabled) in front of Azure App Service or AKS. App Service uses deployment slots for zero-downtime releases. Data tier: Azure SQL with active geo-replication (readable secondary in region 2, automatic failover group with a single connection endpoint). Or Cosmos DB with multi-region writes for global distribution. Caching: Azure Cache for Redis with geo-replication. Storage: Azure Blob Storage with GRS (geo-redundant storage) or GZRS (geo-zone redundant). Messaging: Service Bus Premium with geo-disaster recovery (paired namespaces). Monitoring: Azure Monitor with cross-region Log Analytics workspace, Application Insights for distributed tracing. DNS: Azure DNS with Traffic Manager as an alternative to Front Door for DNS-level routing. Security: Entra ID for auth, Key Vault per region for secrets, DDoS Protection Standard, NSGs, and Azure Firewall. RTO/RPO: Front Door detects regional failure in seconds; database failover within minutes. Design for async replication lag in the secondary region.

cards.question: What are Managed Identities in Azure and why should you use them?
cards.answer: Managed Identities provide an automatically managed identity in Entra ID for Azure resources to authenticate to services that support Entra authentication — eliminating the need to store credentials in code or config. Two types: System-assigned — tied to a specific resource (e.g., a VM or App Service); created and deleted with the resource. User-assigned — created as a standalone Azure resource; can be shared across multiple resources. How it works: Azure provisions a service principal in Entra ID. The Azure instance metadata service (IMDS) provides tokens to the resource at a well-known endpoint (`http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token`). The resource requests a token for a target audience (e.g., `https://database.windows.net/`). No secrets involved — the token rotation is fully managed by Azure. Example: An App Service with a managed identity can access Azure SQL Database, Key Vault secrets, Storage blobs, and Service Bus without any connection strings containing passwords. Just assign the appropriate RBAC role (e.g., Key Vault Secrets User) to the managed identity. Supported by all major Azure SDKs via `DefaultAzureCredential`.

cards.question: Compare Azure App Service, Azure Container Instances (ACI), and AKS for running containerized workloads.
cards.answer: Azure App Service (Web App for Containers) — PaaS for web apps. Supports single-container or Docker Compose (multi-container). Built-in auto-scaling, deployment slots, custom domains, managed TLS, Entra auth. Best for: standard web apps and APIs where you want minimal infrastructure management. Azure Container Instances (ACI) — serverless containers on demand. No cluster to manage. Per-second billing, starts in seconds. Supports Linux and Windows, GPU instances. Limited orchestration (no auto-scaling, no service discovery). Best for: burst workloads, batch jobs, sidecar containers, dev/test, or as AKS virtual node burst targets. AKS — full Kubernetes orchestrator. Complex but most powerful. Node pools, auto-scaling (cluster and pod), service mesh, Helm, gitops, network policies, RBAC. Best for: microservice architectures at scale, teams with Kubernetes expertise, workloads requiring fine-grained control. Decision guide: Start with App Service for simple web workloads. Use ACI for short-lived or bursty containers. Graduate to AKS when you need orchestration, service mesh, or run many interdependent microservices.

cards.question: What happens when you create a resource in Azure — what is the ARM control plane flow?
cards.answer: Every Azure operation goes through Azure Resource Manager (ARM), the control plane. Flow: 1) Client (portal, CLI, SDK, Terraform) sends an HTTPS request to `management.azure.com`. 2) ARM authenticates the caller via Entra ID (OAuth token validation). 3) ARM authorizes the action — checks RBAC role assignments at the applicable scope, evaluates deny assignments, and checks Azure Policy. 4) If Azure Policy is in 'deny' effect and the resource violates it, the request is rejected with an error. 5) ARM routes the request to the appropriate Resource Provider (e.g., `Microsoft.Compute` for VMs, `Microsoft.Storage` for storage accounts). 6) The Resource Provider provisions the resource asynchronously and returns a status. 7) ARM returns the response to the client with a provisioning state (Succeeded, Failed, Creating). For long-running operations, ARM returns a 201/202 with an `Azure-AsyncOperation` URL to poll. All operations are logged in the Activity Log. This single control plane means all tools (CLI, portal, Terraform, Bicep) have identical behavior and policy enforcement.

cards.question: How do you implement zero-trust networking in Azure?
cards.answer: Zero-trust assumes breach and verifies every access request. Azure implementation: Identity layer — Entra ID Conditional Access policies enforce MFA, device compliance, and sign-in risk before granting access. Privileged Identity Management (PIM) for just-in-time admin access. Network layer — Use Private Endpoints to remove public internet exposure for Azure services. NSGs with default-deny and explicit allow rules. Azure Firewall as a central egress point with FQDN filtering and threat intelligence. DDoS Protection Standard. Application layer — Azure Application Gateway WAF with OWASP rules. API Management for API gateway security. Data layer — Encrypt at rest (Azure Storage Service Encryption, TDE for SQL) and in transit (enforce TLS 1.2+). Azure Key Vault for secret, key, and certificate management; never store secrets in code. Endpoint layer — Microsoft Defender for Cloud for VM/container vulnerability assessment. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint for threat detection. Monitoring — Microsoft Sentinel (SIEM) correlates signals across identity, network, and application layers. Continuous access evaluation revokes tokens in real-time when conditions change.

cards.question: What is Azure Service Bus and how does it differ from Azure Queue Storage?
cards.answer: Both are messaging services, but they serve different needs. Azure Queue Storage is a simple, high-volume queue built on Azure Storage. Max message size 64KB, 7-day max TTL (configurable to infinite), at-least-once delivery, no ordering guarantee, no dead-letter queue natively. Very cheap — priced at storage rates. Best for simple decoupling with high message volume. Azure Service Bus is an enterprise message broker. Supports queues (point-to-point) and topics/subscriptions (pub/sub). Max message size 256KB (Standard) or 100MB (Premium). Features: guaranteed FIFO (sessions), exactly-once delivery (peek-lock with deduplication), dead-letter queue, message scheduling (deliver at a future time), transactions, auto-forwarding between queues, and SQL-like subscription filters on topics. Premium tier runs on dedicated resources with predictable latency. Best for enterprise integration, workflows requiring ordering and exactly-once semantics, or pub/sub fan-out. Choose Queue Storage for simple, high-volume, cost-sensitive scenarios. Choose Service Bus when you need enterprise features.

cards.question: What is Azure Key Vault and how do applications authenticate to it?
cards.answer: Azure Key Vault centrally manages secrets (connection strings, API keys), cryptographic keys (RSA/EC for encrypt/sign), and certificates (auto-renewal with DigiCert/GlobalSign). Access control: Use RBAC (recommended) or vault access policies. RBAC roles include Key Vault Secrets User (read secrets), Key Vault Crypto Officer (manage keys), Key Vault Administrator (full control). Applications authenticate using Managed Identity (preferred — no credentials to manage) or Service Principal (client ID + client secret or certificate). In code, use `DefaultAzureCredential` from the Azure Identity SDK — it tries managed identity first, then environment variables, then Azure CLI credentials. Key Vault supports soft-delete (recoverable for 7-90 days) and purge protection (prevents permanent deletion during retention period). For high-security scenarios, use Key Vault Managed HSM (FIPS 140-2 Level 3, single-tenant HSM). Key Vault references in App Service/Functions let you load secrets directly into app settings without code changes: `@Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=https://myvault.vault.azure.net/secrets/mySecret/)`.

cards.question: How does Azure Policy work and what are initiatives?
cards.answer: Azure Policy evaluates resources against rules and enforces organizational standards. A policy definition specifies a condition (IF) and an effect (THEN). Effects: Deny — block non-compliant resource creation/modification. Audit — allow but flag as non-compliant. Append — add fields (e.g., force tags). Modify — add/update/remove properties on existing resources (e.g., add a tag). DeployIfNotExists — deploy a remediation resource if missing (e.g., deploy diagnostic settings). AuditIfNotExists — audit if a related resource doesn't exist. Policies are assigned at a scope (management group, subscription, resource group). An Initiative (policy set) groups multiple policies for assignment as a unit — e.g., the CIS Microsoft Azure Foundations Benchmark initiative bundles 200+ policies. Compliance dashboard shows the percentage of compliant resources. Remediation tasks can fix existing non-compliant resources (for Modify and DeployIfNotExists effects). Custom policies use JSON with `policyRule`, `parameters`, and `mode` (All or Indexed). Azure Policy integrates with AKS via Azure Policy for Kubernetes (Gatekeeper) to enforce pod security standards.

cards.question: What are Azure Logic Apps and how do they compare to Azure Functions for integration workflows?
cards.answer: Logic Apps is a low-code/no-code workflow orchestration service with 400+ connectors to SaaS and enterprise systems (Office 365, Salesforce, SAP, ServiceNow, Dynamics 365, Twitter). Workflows are defined visually in a designer or as JSON (Workflow Definition Language). Logic Apps excel at: integration between systems, B2B workflows (EDI, AS2, X12), long-running approval workflows, and connecting to enterprise APIs. Pricing: Consumption (per trigger/action execution) or Standard (dedicated compute, VNet integration, stateful and stateless workflows). Azure Functions is code-first, optimized for compute-intensive event processing. Functions excel at: data transformation, real-time stream processing, API backends, and any scenario requiring custom code logic. Use Logic Apps when: you need to connect many SaaS/enterprise systems with minimal code, the workflow is primarily orchestration and data routing, or business users need to understand the flow. Use Functions when: you need custom business logic, high-performance processing, or full code control. They complement each other — Logic Apps often call Functions for complex processing steps within a workflow.

cards.question: Explain Azure Landing Zones and the Cloud Adoption Framework.
cards.answer: The Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) is Microsoft's guidance for cloud adoption with phases: Strategy (motivations, business outcomes), Plan (digital estate assessment, migration plan), Ready (landing zone setup), Adopt (migrate or innovate), Govern (policy, cost, security baselines), and Manage (operations, monitoring). An Azure Landing Zone is the foundation environment — a scalable, multi-account architecture following CAF best practices. Key components: Management Group hierarchy (Root > Platform > Workload > Sandbox), with Subscriptions nested under MGs. Platform landing zone: Identity subscription (Entra ID Connect, domain controllers), Management subscription (Log Analytics, Automation, Monitor), Connectivity subscription (hub VNet, VPN/ExpressRoute gateways, Azure Firewall, DNS). Application landing zones: separate subscriptions per workload/team with standardized networking (spoke VNets peered to hub). Azure Policy assignments at MG level enforce guardrails (allowed regions, required tags, encryption). Deploy using: Azure Landing Zone Accelerator (portal), Bicep modules, or Terraform modules (CAF enterprise-scale). This provides: blast radius isolation, clear cost boundaries, consistent governance, and network segmentation from day one.

cards.question: What is Azure Event Hub and when would you use it over Service Bus?
cards.answer: Azure Event Hubs is a big data streaming platform and event ingestion service capable of receiving millions of events per second. It uses a partitioned consumer model — events are appended to partitions (append-only logs) and consumers read at their own pace using consumer groups (each group maintains its own offset). Retention: 1-90 days (Standard/Premium) or unlimited (Event Hubs Capture to Blob/Data Lake). Event Hubs Capture automatically writes events to Azure Storage in Avro format for batch analytics. Kafka compatibility: Event Hubs exposes a Kafka-compatible endpoint — existing Kafka producers/consumers work with zero code changes. Use Event Hubs when: you have high-volume telemetry/event streams (IoT, clickstream, logs), need replay capability, or want Kafka compatibility on a managed service. Use Service Bus when: you need traditional enterprise messaging — guaranteed delivery, FIFO, dead-letter queues, transactions, request-reply patterns, or complex topic subscription filters. Key difference: Event Hubs is for stream processing (events are not deleted after reading); Service Bus is for message processing (messages are consumed and removed).

cards.question: How do you secure an Azure App Service web application end to end?
cards.answer: Network: Deploy into a VNet using VNet Integration (outbound) and Private Endpoints (inbound) — remove public access entirely. Use Application Gateway or Front Door with WAF in front. Set access restrictions to allow traffic only from the gateway's IP or subnet. Identity: Enable Entra ID authentication (Easy Auth) for user-facing apps. Use Managed Identity for backend service-to-service calls (Key Vault, SQL, Storage). Secrets: Store all secrets in Key Vault; reference them in App Settings using Key Vault references (`@Microsoft.KeyVault(...)`). Never commit secrets to source control. TLS: Enforce HTTPS-only, set minimum TLS version to 1.2, use managed certificates or ACM. App config: Disable FTP, disable remote debugging in production, enable diagnostic logging to Log Analytics. Runtime: Keep the platform and dependencies updated; enable Defender for App Service for runtime threat detection. CORS: Configure allowed origins explicitly. Headers: Add security headers (X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options) via web.config or middleware.
