Header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=2 or custom X-API-Version header): cleanest from a REST purist perspective, resource URIs stay stable, supports content negotiation. Downside: invisible in browser address bars, harder to test with curl, requires header-aware routing. Best practice: use URL path versioning for major breaking changes (v1 vs v2), and use additive/backward-compatible changes (new fields, optional parameters) to minimize the need for new versions. Never remove fields — deprecate them. Support at least N-1 versions concurrently.

cards.question: Compare cursor-based and offset-based pagination. When should you use each?
cards.answer: Offset-based (GET /items?offset=20&limit=10): simple to implement (SQL OFFSET/LIMIT), supports jumping to arbitrary pages, and users can see total count. Downsides: O(n) database performance as offset grows (DB must scan and skip rows), inconsistent results when data changes between pages (items shift, causing duplicates or missing records). Cursor-based (GET /items?cursor=eyJpZCI6MTAwfQ&limit=10): the cursor is an opaque token encoding the last item's sort key. The DB queries WHERE id > :cursor LIMIT 10, which is O(1) using an index. Consistent results even as data changes. Downsides: cannot jump to page N, harder to show total count. Use offset for small datasets with static data (admin dashboards, search results). Use cursor for large/real-time datasets (social feeds, event logs, infinite scroll). Keyset pagination (WHERE created_at > :last_created AND id > :last_id) is the SQL implementation of cursor-based. Always return a 'next' link or cursor in the response to enable stateless iteration.

cards.question: How do you implement rate limiting for an API? What algorithms exist?
cards.answer: Rate limiting protects APIs from abuse and ensures fair resource usage. Common algorithms: (1) Fixed window — count requests per fixed time window (e.g., 100/minute). Simple but bursty at window edges (200 requests possible in 1 second spanning two windows). (2) Sliding window log — store timestamps of each request, count those within the window. Accurate but memory-intensive. (3) Sliding window counter — hybrid: uses fixed windows but interpolates based on the overlap with the current sliding window. Good balance of accuracy and memory. (4) Token bucket — bucket holds N tokens, refills at a fixed rate, each request consumes one token. Allows bursts up to bucket capacity while enforcing average rate. (5) Leaky bucket — requests queue up and are processed at a fixed rate, excess requests are dropped. Smoothest output rate. Implementation: use Redis (INCR with EXPIRE for fixed window, or sorted sets for sliding log). Return 429 with headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset, and Retry-After. Apply per-user, per-IP, or per-API-key.

cards.question: What is OAuth 2.0, and what are the main grant types?
cards.answer: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework that lets third-party applications access resources on behalf of a user without exposing their credentials. Main grant types: (1) Authorization Code — the standard for web/mobile apps. User is redirected to the auth server, grants consent, receives an auth code, which the app exchanges (server-side) for access + refresh tokens. Most secure for confidential clients. (2) Authorization Code with PKCE — required for public clients (SPAs, mobile apps) since they cannot store a client secret. Uses a code_verifier/code_challenge to prevent interception attacks. (3) Client Credentials — machine-to-machine auth with no user context. The app authenticates directly with client_id/client_secret and gets an access token. (4) Device Code — for input-limited devices (TVs, CLIs). User authenticates on a separate device. Implicit grant and Resource Owner Password grant are deprecated in OAuth 2.1 due to security issues. Always use short-lived access tokens (15-60 min) with refresh tokens for long-lived sessions.

cards.question: What is a JWT, and what are the security considerations when using it for API auth?
cards.answer: A JWT (JSON Web Token) is a compact, URL-safe token with three Base64-encoded parts: header (algorithm, type), payload (claims like sub, exp, iat, custom data), and signature (HMAC or RSA/ECDSA). Security considerations: (1) Always use asymmetric signing (RS256/ES256) for distributed systems — the auth server signs with a private key, API servers verify with the public key. HS256 requires sharing the secret with every service. (2) Always validate: signature, exp (expiration), iss (issuer), aud (audience). (3) Never store sensitive data in the payload — it is encoded, not encrypted (use JWE if encryption is needed). (4) Keep tokens short-lived (15 min) — JWTs cannot be revoked once issued. For revocation, maintain a deny-list in Redis or use token introspection. (5) Store in HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookies (not localStorage) to prevent XSS theft. (6) Protect against 'none' algorithm attack — explicitly whitelist allowed algorithms. (7) Use 'kid' (key ID) for key rotation without downtime.

cards.question: What is the OpenAPI Specification, and how does it improve API development?
cards.answer: The OpenAPI Specification (formerly Swagger) is a language-agnostic standard for describing REST APIs in YAML or JSON. It defines endpoints, HTTP methods, request/response schemas (using JSON Schema), authentication methods, and error formats. Benefits: (1) Auto-generated interactive documentation (Swagger UI, Redoc) where developers can try API calls in the browser. (2) Client SDK generation (openapi-generator) for 30+ languages — eliminates manual HTTP client code. (3) Server stub generation to scaffold API implementations. (4) Contract-first development — design the API spec first, then implement, ensuring the contract is agreed upon before coding begins. (5) Automated testing — tools like Schemathesis generate fuzz tests from the spec. (6) API gateway integration — AWS API Gateway, Kong, and Apigee can import OpenAPI specs to configure routes, validation, and throttling. Best practice: version-control your OpenAPI spec alongside code, use CI to validate that the implementation matches the spec (e.g., openapi-diff for breaking changes).

cards.question: What is RFC 7807 (Problem Details for HTTP APIs), and why should you use it?
cards.answer: RFC 7807 defines a standard JSON format for API error responses, replacing ad-hoc error structures. The response uses Content-Type: application/problem+json and includes: 'type' (URI identifying the error type, e.g., /errors/insufficient-funds), 'title' (human-readable summary), 'status' (HTTP status code), 'detail' (specific explanation for this occurrence), and 'instance' (URI identifying the specific occurrence for log correlation). You can add extension fields for domain-specific data (e.g., 'balance': 30, 'cost': 50). Benefits: clients can parse errors uniformly across all endpoints, error types are machine-readable and linkable to documentation, and it eliminates the inconsistency of different teams inventing different error formats. Example: {"type": "/errors/validation", "title": "Validation Error", "status": 422, "detail": "'email' must be a valid email address", "instance": "/logs/abc-123", "errors": [{"field": "email", "message": "invalid format"}]}. Supported natively by ASP.NET and Spring Boot.

cards.question: How do you design an idempotent API, and why does it matter?
cards.answer: An idempotent API produces the same result regardless of how many times the same request is made — critical for handling retries from network failures, client timeouts, or duplicate webhook deliveries. GET, PUT, and DELETE are inherently idempotent by HTTP spec. POST is not — making it idempotent requires explicit design. Implementation: (1) Idempotency keys — the client sends a unique key (UUID) in a header (e.g., Idempotency-Key). The server stores the key with the response in Redis/DB. On duplicate requests, return the stored response without re-executing. Stripe uses this pattern. (2) Conditional requests — use If-Match with ETags so the server only processes the request if the resource version matches. (3) Natural idempotency — design operations as absolute rather than relative ('set balance to 100' instead of 'add 50 to balance'). Key details: idempotency keys should expire (24-48 hours), the key-response mapping must be stored atomically with the operation, and the server should return 409 Conflict if the same key is used with different request bodies.

cards.question: What is API gateway pattern, and what responsibilities does it handle?
cards.answer: An API gateway is a single entry point that sits between clients and backend services, handling cross-cutting concerns. Responsibilities: (1) Request routing — maps public endpoints to internal microservice URLs, potentially aggregating multiple services into one client call (BFF pattern). (2) Authentication/authorization — validates tokens centrally so individual services do not need to. (3) Rate limiting and throttling — protects backends from overload. (4) Request/response transformation — converting protocols (REST to gRPC), adding/removing headers, reshaping payloads. (5) Caching — caches GET responses at the edge. (6) Load balancing — distributes across service instances. (7) Observability — centralized logging, metrics, and distributed tracing injection. Popular implementations: AWS API Gateway (serverless, managed), Kong (open-source, Lua plugins), Envoy (high-performance, gRPC-native), Traefik (container-native, auto-discovery). Anti-pattern: putting business logic in the gateway — it should remain a thin routing/policy layer. For multiple client types, use the Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) variant where each client (web, mobile) has its own gateway.

cards.question: How do you handle API backward compatibility and deprecation?
cards.answer: Backward-compatible changes (safe to make without versioning): adding new optional fields to responses, adding new optional query parameters, adding new endpoints, adding new enum values (if clients handle unknown values). Breaking changes (require versioning): removing or renaming fields, changing field types, making optional fields required, changing URL structure, altering error formats. Deprecation process: (1) Mark deprecated fields/endpoints in OpenAPI spec with 'deprecated: true'. (2) Return Deprecation and Sunset headers (RFC 8594) with the retirement date. (3) Log usage of deprecated endpoints to identify affected consumers. (4) Communicate via changelog, developer portal, and email with a migration guide. (5) Maintain the deprecated version for a defined period (typically 6-12 months). (6) Monitor traffic — do not remove until usage drops to near zero. Use expand-and-contract for field renames: add the new field, populate both old and new, migrate consumers, then remove the old field. API linting tools (Spectral) can enforce these rules in CI.

cards.question: What is the BFF (Backend for Frontend) pattern, and when should you use it?
cards.answer: BFF creates a dedicated backend service for each frontend client (web, mobile, third-party). Instead of one generic API serving all clients, each BFF tailors responses to its client's needs. Example: a mobile BFF returns minimal payloads optimized for bandwidth and pre-aggregates data from multiple services into a single call, while a web BFF returns richer data for desktop layouts. Use BFF when: (1) Different clients need significantly different data shapes or aggregation logic. (2) A single API would become bloated with client-specific query parameters and conditional logic. (3) Different teams own different clients and want independent deployment cycles. Each BFF is owned by the frontend team it serves. Implementation: BFFs sit behind an API gateway that handles auth and routing, and call internal microservices via gRPC or REST. Anti-patterns: duplicating business logic across BFFs (extract into shared services), letting BFFs grow into monoliths, or creating too many BFFs (one per client type is typical, not one per feature).

cards.question: How do you design a filtering and sorting API for a collection endpoint?
cards.answer: Standard patterns: (1) Simple filtering — use query parameters matching field names: GET /products?category=electronics&in_stock=true. (2) Comparison operators — use bracket syntax: ?price[gte]=10&price[lte]=100 or LHS brackets: ?filter[price][gte]=10. (3) Complex filtering — for AND/OR logic, use a filter query language: ?filter=category eq 'electronics' and (price lt 100 or rating gte 4). OData and SCIM define standard filter grammars. (4) Sorting — use a sort parameter: ?sort=price,-created_at (prefix with - for descending). Support multiple sort fields with comma separation. (5) Field selection (sparse fieldsets) — ?fields=id,name,price to reduce payload size. JSON:API standardizes this as ?fields[products]=id,name. Implementation details: validate all filter/sort fields against an allowlist to prevent SQL injection. Index commonly filtered fields. Default to sensible sorts (e.g., -created_at). Always combine filtering with pagination. Document all supported operators in your OpenAPI spec.

cards.question: What is content negotiation in REST APIs, and how does it work?
cards.answer: Content negotiation allows a client to request the format it prefers for a response, and the server to indicate what it can provide. The client sends an Accept header (e.g., Accept: application/json, application/xml;q=0.9) with quality factors indicating preference. The server inspects this, selects the best match from its supported formats, and sets the Content-Type header on the response. If no match exists, the server returns 406 Not Acceptable. This also applies to: Accept-Language (localization), Accept-Encoding (compression like gzip, br), and Accept-Charset. For API versioning, vendor media types are used: Accept: application/vnd.myapi.v2+json. In practice, most modern APIs only support JSON. Content negotiation becomes more valuable for: supporting both JSON and Protocol Buffers (for performance-sensitive clients), returning CSV for export endpoints, or supporting HAL/JSON:API hypermedia formats. Implement it in middleware rather than in each endpoint handler.

cards.question: Design an API for a webhook system. What reliability guarantees should you provide?
cards.answer: Webhook API design: (1) Registration endpoint — POST /webhooks with {url, events: ['order.created', 'payment.failed'], secret}. Return the webhook ID and signing secret. (2) Payload delivery — POST to the registered URL with a JSON body containing: event type, timestamp, idempotency key (event ID), and the event data. Sign the payload with HMAC-SHA256 using the shared secret in a header (e.g., X-Webhook-Signature). (3) Reliability guarantees: at-least-once delivery with exponential backoff retries (e.g., 5 retries over 24 hours at 1min, 5min, 30min, 2hr, 24hr). The consumer must be idempotent (use the event ID to deduplicate). (4) Return requirements — treat 2xx as success, anything else as failure triggering retry. (5) Management endpoints — GET /webhooks (list), GET /webhooks/:id/deliveries (delivery log with status codes and response times), POST /webhooks/:id/test (send a test event). (6) Security — validate the receiving URL (no localhost/internal IPs), enforce HTTPS, sign payloads, set a timeout (5-30s), and include a timestamp to prevent replay attacks. Disable webhooks after N consecutive failures and notify the developer.

cards.question: What are ETags and conditional requests, and how do they improve API performance?
cards.answer: An ETag (Entity Tag) is a hash or version identifier for a resource's current state, returned in the ETag response header. Clients use it in subsequent requests for two purposes: (1) Cache validation — client sends If-None-Match: "etag-value" with a GET. If the resource has not changed, the server returns 304 Not Modified with no body, saving bandwidth and processing. (2) Optimistic concurrency — client sends If-Match: "etag-value" with PUT/PATCH/DELETE. The server only processes the request if the ETag matches the current version, preventing lost updates when two clients edit simultaneously. If the ETag does not match, return 412 Precondition Failed. Strong ETags ("abc123") indicate byte-for-byte identical responses. Weak ETags (W/"abc123") indicate semantically equivalent responses (minor formatting differences allowed). Generate ETags from: content hash (MD5/SHA of response body), database row version/updated_at timestamp, or a version counter. Combine with Cache-Control headers (max-age, stale-while-revalidate) for a complete caching strategy.

cards.question: What is the difference between `extends` in a generic constraint vs a conditional type?
cards.answer: In a generic constraint (`T extends string`), `extends` restricts T to subtypes of string — it's a bound. In a conditional type (`T extends string ? A : B`), `extends` acts as a type-level ternary that checks assignability at the type level. Crucially, conditional types distribute over unions: `(string | number) extends string ? A : B` evaluates each union member separately when T is a naked type parameter, producing `A | B` rather than just `B`.

cards.question: Explain the `infer` keyword in TypeScript. Give an example extracting the return type of a function.
cards.answer: The `infer` keyword declares a type variable inside a conditional type's extends clause that TypeScript infers from context. Example: `type ReturnOf<T> = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never;` — if T is a function type, R captures its return type. This is exactly how the built-in `ReturnType<T>` utility works. `infer` can also extract element types from arrays (`T extends (infer U)[] ? U : never`), promise values, and template literal segments.

cards.question: What are mapped types? Write a mapped type that makes all properties of an object optional and readonly.
cards.answer: Mapped types iterate over keys of a type to produce a new type: `type ReadonlyPartial<T> = { readonly [K in keyof T]?: T[K] };`. The `in keyof` iterates over each property key, `readonly` adds immutability, and `?` makes each property optional. You can also remap keys using `as`: `{ [K in keyof T as Uppercase<string & K>]: T[K] }`. Built-in mapped types include `Partial`, `Required`, `Readonly`, and `Record`.

cards.question: Compare `Pick<T, K>`, `Omit<T, K>`, and `Extract<T, U>`. When would you use each?
cards.answer: `Pick<T, K>` creates a type with only the specified keys from T — use it to create a subset DTO (e.g., `Pick<User, 'id' | 'name'>`). `Omit<T, K>` creates a type excluding the specified keys — use it to strip sensitive fields (e.g., `Omit<User, 'password'>`). `Extract<T, U>` filters a union type, keeping only members assignable to U — e.g., `Extract<string | number | boolean, string | number>` yields `string | number`. Pick/Omit work on object keys; Extract works on union members.

cards.question: What does the `satisfies` operator do and how does it differ from a type annotation?
cards.answer: The `satisfies` operator (TS 4.9+) validates that an expression matches a type without widening it. With `const config: Config = { ... }`, the type of `config` is exactly `Config`, losing literal types. With `const config = { ... } satisfies Config`, TypeScript verifies conformance to Config but preserves the narrower inferred type — so `config.mode` might be `'dark'` (literal) instead of `string`. This gives you both type safety AND precise inference, which is ideal for configuration objects and constants.

cards.question: Explain discriminated unions and exhaustive checking. How do you ensure all cases are handled?
cards.answer: A discriminated union has a common literal property (the discriminant) that TypeScript uses for narrowing: `type Shape = { kind: 'circle'; radius: number } | { kind: 'square'; side: number }`. In a switch on `shape.kind`, each case narrows to the specific variant. For exhaustive checking, add a default case: `default: const _exhaustive: never = shape; return _exhaustive;`. If you add a new variant to the union but forget to handle it, the assignment to `never` fails at compile time. This pattern is critical for state machines and action reducers.

cards.question: What is a user-defined type guard? Write one that narrows `unknown` to a specific interface.
cards.answer: A type guard is a function whose return type is a type predicate (`param is Type`), telling TypeScript to narrow the type in the truthy branch. Example: `function isUser(val: unknown): val is User { return typeof val === 'object' && val !== null && 'id' in val && typeof (val as any).id === 'string'; }`. After `if (isUser(data))`, TypeScript knows `data` is `User`. Type guards are essential for validating API responses, deserializing JSON, and working with `unknown` instead of `any`. The `asserts param is Type` variant throws instead of returning false.

cards.question: What are branded types (nominal typing) in TypeScript and why would you use them?
cards.answer: TypeScript uses structural typing, so `type USD = number` and `type EUR = number` are interchangeable. Branded types simulate nominal typing: `type USD = number & { __brand: 'USD' }; type EUR = number & { __brand: 'EUR' };`. Now passing a USD where EUR is expected is a compile error. You create values via constructor functions: `const usd = (n: number): USD => n as USD;`. Use cases include preventing unit mix-ups (pixels vs rem, user IDs vs post IDs), enforcing validated data (sanitized HTML, validated email), and making impossible states unrepresentable at the type level.

cards.question: How does Zod complement TypeScript's type system? Compare it to manual type guards.
cards.answer: Zod provides runtime schema validation that automatically infers TypeScript types: `const UserSchema = z.object({ name: z.string(), age: z.number().min(0) }); type User = z.infer<typeof UserSchema>;`. Unlike manual type guards, Zod gives you: (1) single source of truth for both runtime validation and static types, (2) composable schemas with transforms and refinements, (3) detailed error messages with paths, (4) no type drift between runtime checks and TS types. Manual guards require writing validation logic AND a separate type definition that can fall out of sync. Zod is ideal at API boundaries, form validation, and environment variable parsing.

cards.question: Design a type-safe builder pattern in TypeScript that prevents calling `.build()` before all required fields are set.
cards.answer: Use a generic that tracks which fields have been set: `class Builder<T extends Partial<Config>> { private data: T; set<K extends keyof Config>(key: K, val: Config[K]): Builder<T & Record<K, Config[K]>> { (this.data as any)[key] = val; return this as any; } build(this: Builder<Required<Config>>): Config { return this.data as Config; } }`. The generic T starts as `{}` and accumulates fields. The `build` method's `this` parameter requires T to be `Required<Config>`, so calling `.build()` before setting all fields is a compile error. This guarantees correctness without runtime checks.

cards.question: What are template literal types and how can they be used for type-safe routing?
cards.answer: Template literal types (TS 4.1+) allow string manipulation at the type level: `type Route = `/users/${string}/posts/${number}`;`. A function `navigate(path: Route)` rejects `'/users/123'` but accepts `'/users/123/posts/42'`. Combined with `infer`, you can parse route params: `type Params<T> = T extends `/${infer Seg}/${infer Rest}` ? Seg | Params<`/${Rest}`> : never;`. Libraries like tRPC and Hono use template literals for type-safe API routes, ensuring client and server route definitions stay in sync at compile time.

cards.question: Explain the differences between `tsconfig.json` options `strict`, `strictNullChecks`, and `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`.
cards.answer: `strict: true` is an umbrella that enables ~10 strict flags including `strictNullChecks`, `strictFunctionTypes`, `strictBindCallApply`, `noImplicitAny`, and `noImplicitThis`. `strictNullChecks` makes `null` and `undefined` their own types instead of assignable to everything — so `string` no longer includes `null`. `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` (TS 4.1+) adds `undefined` to index signatures: `const arr: string[] = []; arr[0]` becomes `string | undefined` instead of `string`. This catches a huge class of runtime errors but requires more null checks. A production codebase should enable all three.

cards.question: What is the difference between ESM (`import/export`) and CJS (`require/module.exports`) in a TypeScript context? How does `moduleResolution` affect things?
cards.answer: ESM is statically analyzable (imports at top level, tree-shakeable), while CJS is dynamic (require at runtime, synchronous). In TS, `module: 'esnext'` emits ESM, `module: 'commonjs'` emits CJS. `moduleResolution: 'node16'` or `'bundler'` determines how TS resolves import paths — `'bundler'` allows extensionless imports like bundlers do, while `'node16'` requires explicit `.js` extensions in ESM (even for `.ts` files). Common pitfalls: `default` import interop (CJS has no default export), `__dirname` unavailable in ESM (use `import.meta.url`), and dual-package hazard where the same package loads twice.

cards.question: What are TC39 decorators (Stage 3) and how do they differ from legacy/experimental TypeScript decorators?
cards.answer: Legacy TS decorators (`experimentalDecorators: true`) use `reflect-metadata` and receive `(target, propertyKey, descriptor)`. TC39 decorators (TS 5.0+, no flag needed) receive a `(value, context)` pair where context includes `kind`, `name`, `static`, `private`, and `addInitializer`. Key differences: TC39 decorators have no `reflect-metadata`, class field decorators get the initializer value (not a descriptor), and decorator order/timing changed. TC39 decorators cannot modify the type of the decorated element. Migration requires rewriting decorators — frameworks like Angular and NestJS are migrating incrementally.

cards.question: How do project references and incremental compilation improve TypeScript build performance?
cards.answer: Project references (`references` in tsconfig.json + `composite: true`) split a monorepo into sub-projects that compile independently. `tsc --build` only recompiles projects whose dependencies changed, using `.tsbuildinfo` files to track what's stale. Incremental compilation (`incremental: true`) caches the dependency graph within a single project. Together they transform O(total codebase) rebuilds into O(changed project) — often 10-50x faster. Best practices: put shared types in a `packages/types` project, use `declarationMap: true` for go-to-definition across projects, and run `tsc --build --watch` for dev.

cards.question: What is the `const` type parameter modifier in TypeScript 5.0 and when is it useful?
cards.answer: The `const` modifier on a generic parameter (`function foo<const T>(arg: T)`) tells TypeScript to infer the narrowest possible literal type, as if the caller had written `as const`. Without it, `foo(['a', 'b'])` infers `string[]`; with it, TypeScript infers `readonly ['a', 'b']`. This is critical for type-safe API builders, route definitions, and configuration objects where you need exact literal types without forcing callers to add `as const` at every call site. It replaces the verbose pattern of overloads or manual narrowing.

cards.question: Explain variance in TypeScript. What are covariance, contravariance, and how does `strictFunctionTypes` enforce them?
cards.answer: Covariance means if `Dog extends Animal`, then `Array<Dog>` is assignable to `Array<Animal>` — subtypes flow in the same direction. Contravariance reverses: function parameter types are contravariant, meaning `(animal: Animal) => void` is assignable to `(dog: Dog) => void` but NOT the reverse, because the function must handle all Animals, not just Dogs. Before `strictFunctionTypes`, TS treated function parameters as bivariant (unsafe). With it enabled, method signatures (`foo(x: T): void` in interfaces) remain bivariant for legacy compatibility, but function properties (`foo: (x: T) => void`) are properly contravariant. TS 4.7 added `in`/`out` variance annotations for explicit control.

cards.question: What is `keyof` and how does it interact with index signatures and mapped types?
cards.answer: `keyof T` produces a union of T's known property keys. For `{ name: string; age: number }`, `keyof` gives `'name' | 'age'`. With an index signature `{ [key: string]: unknown }`, `keyof` returns `string | number` (because numeric keys are a subset of string keys in JS). In mapped types, `keyof` drives iteration: `{ [K in keyof T]: SomeTransform<T[K]> }`. Combined with `typeof`, you can extract keys from runtime objects: `const obj = { a: 1, b: 2 } as const; type Keys = keyof typeof obj; // 'a' | 'b'`. This is foundational for type-safe property access patterns like `function get<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K): T[K]`.

cards.question: How would you type a function that accepts any object and returns a deep readonly version of it?
cards.answer: Use a recursive conditional mapped type: `type DeepReadonly<T> = T extends Function ? T : T extends object ? { readonly [K in keyof T]: DeepReadonly<T[K]> } : T;`. The function signature: `function freeze<T>(obj: T): DeepReadonly<T> { return Object.freeze(obj) as DeepReadonly<T>; }`. The Function check prevents making callable signatures readonly. For arrays, this automatically produces `readonly` arrays since arrays are objects. For deep objects, each nested level gets `readonly` applied recursively. This is a common interview question testing recursive type definitions and understanding of TypeScript's structural type resolution with circular types.

cards.question: What is the `Record<K, V>` utility type and how does it differ from an index signature?
cards.answer: `Record<K, V>` is a mapped type: `{ [P in K]: V }`. With `Record<string, number>`, it's equivalent to `{ [key: string]: number }`. The key difference is K can be a union of literals: `Record<'GET' | 'POST' | 'PUT', Handler>` creates an object with exactly those three keys, all required. An index signature `{ [key: string]: Handler }` allows any string key and doesn't require specific ones. Use `Record` when you have a known finite set of keys (HTTP methods, status codes, feature flags) and index signatures when keys are truly dynamic.

cards.question: What happens when you use `as const` on an object literal? How does it affect type inference?
cards.answer: `as const` applies three effects recursively: (1) all properties become `readonly`, (2) all literal values keep their narrowest type (`'hello'` instead of `string`, `42` instead of `number`), and (3) arrays become `readonly` tuples with fixed length. Example: `const config = { endpoint: '/api', retries: 3, methods: ['GET', 'POST'] } as const;` infers `{ readonly endpoint: '/api'; readonly retries: 3; readonly methods: readonly ['GET', 'POST'] }`. Without `as const`, it would be `{ endpoint: string; retries: number; methods: string[] }`. This is essential for deriving union types from objects: `typeof config.methods[number]` gives `'GET' | 'POST'`.

cards.question: Design a type-safe event emitter in TypeScript where event names and payload types are enforced at compile time.
cards.answer: Define an event map interface and constrain the emitter generically: `interface EventMap { login: { userId: string }; error: { code: number; message: string }; } class Emitter<E extends Record<string, any>> { private handlers = new Map<string, Set<Function>>(); on<K extends keyof E>(event: K, handler: (payload: E[K]) => void): void { if (!this.handlers.has(event as string)) this.handlers.set(event as string, new Set()); this.handlers.get(event as string)!.add(handler); } emit<K extends keyof E>(event: K, payload: E[K]): void { this.handlers.get(event as string)?.forEach(h => h(payload)); } }`. Now `emitter.emit('login', { userId: '123' })` compiles but `emitter.emit('login', { code: 1 })` fails. This pattern is used in Socket.IO, tRPC, and Prisma for type-safe APIs.

cards.question: What is SQL injection and how do parameterized queries prevent it?
cards.answer: SQL injection occurs when user input is concatenated directly into SQL strings, allowing attackers to modify query logic. Example: input `' OR 1=1 --` in a login form transforms `SELECT * FROM users WHERE name='input'` into a query that returns all rows. Parameterized queries (prepared statements) prevent this by separating SQL structure from data: `db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = $1', [userInput])`. The database treats the parameter as a literal value, never as SQL syntax, regardless of its content. This is OWASP A03:2021 (Injection). ORMs like Drizzle, Prisma, and SQLAlchemy use parameterized queries by default.

cards.question: Explain the difference between reflected XSS, stored XSS, and DOM-based XSS.
cards.answer: Reflected XSS: malicious script is in the URL or request and reflected back in the server response (e.g., search query echoed unescaped). Stored XSS: the payload is persisted in the database (e.g., a forum comment with `<script>`) and served to every visitor. DOM-based XSS: the vulnerability is entirely client-side — JavaScript reads from an attacker-controlled source (like `location.hash`) and writes to a sink (`innerHTML`). Mitigations: output encoding (HTML entity escaping), Content Security Policy headers, using `textContent` instead of `innerHTML`, and frameworks like React that auto-escape by default. Stored XSS is the most dangerous because it affects all users without social engineering.

cards.question: What is CSRF and how do anti-CSRF tokens work? Why don't SPAs need traditional CSRF protection?
cards.answer: CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) tricks a user's browser into making an authenticated request to a different site. Example: a hidden form on `evil.com` POSTs to `bank.com/transfer` using the user's existing session cookie. Anti-CSRF tokens work by embedding a random token in forms that the server validates — since the attacker can't read the token from a cross-origin page, they can't forge valid requests. SPAs typically use `Authorization: Bearer ` headers instead of cookies. Since custom headers can't be set by cross-origin form submissions or `` tags, the request can only originate from JavaScript running on the same origin. SameSite cookies (`SameSite=Lax/Strict`) also mitigate CSRF.

cards.question: What is SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery) and why is it in the OWASP Top 10?
cards.answer: SSRF occurs when an attacker can make the server issue HTTP requests to arbitrary URLs. If a server fetches a user-supplied URL (e.g., webhook URL, image proxy), an attacker can target `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (AWS metadata), internal services (`http://localhost:6379` for Redis), or cloud IAM credentials. It's OWASP A10:2021 because cloud environments exposed metadata endpoints, and it was used in the 2019 Capital One breach. Mitigations: allowlist permitted domains, block RFC 1918/link-local IPs, use a dedicated egress proxy, disable HTTP redirects to internal IPs, and run the fetching service in a network-isolated sandbox.

cards.question: Compare bcrypt, scrypt, and Argon2 for password hashing. Which would you choose for a new application?
cards.answer: All three are adaptive key derivation functions designed to be slow. Bcrypt (1999) uses a cost factor for CPU hardness but is limited to 72-byte passwords and is only CPU-hard (vulnerable to GPU/ASIC attacks). Scrypt (2009) adds memory-hardness via a large memory array, making GPU attacks expensive. Argon2 (2015 Password Hashing Competition winner) has two variants: Argon2id (recommended, hybrid of data-dependent and data-independent memory access) provides both GPU resistance and side-channel resistance. Choose Argon2id with parameters: minimum 64MB memory, 3 iterations, 4 parallelism. If unavailable, bcrypt with cost factor 12+ is still solid. Never use MD5, SHA-256, or PBKDF2-SHA1 alone.

cards.question: What is Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)? Compare TOTP, WebAuthn/FIDO2, and SMS-based 2FA.
cards.answer: MFA requires two or more factors: something you know (password), something you have (phone/key), something you are (biometric). TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password, RFC 6238) generates 6-digit codes from a shared secret — phishable if the user enters the code on a fake site. SMS 2FA sends codes via text — vulnerable to SIM swapping, SS7 attacks, and phishing. WebAuthn/FIDO2 uses public-key cryptography with a hardware authenticator (YubiKey) or platform authenticator (TouchID/FaceID) — it's phishing-resistant because the browser binds the credential to the exact origin. Ranking: WebAuthn > TOTP > SMS. NIST SP 800-63B deprecated SMS as a standalone second factor.

cards.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization? Explain RBAC vs ABAC.
cards.answer: Authentication (AuthN) verifies identity — 'who are you?' (login, OAuth, certificates). Authorization (AuthZ) determines permissions — 'what can you do?' (access control). RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) assigns permissions to roles (admin, editor, viewer), and users get roles. Simple but rigid — adding fine-grained rules leads to role explosion. ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control) evaluates policies based on attributes of the subject, resource, action, and environment: 'allow if user.department == resource.department AND time.hour BETWEEN 9-17'. ABAC is more flexible but harder to audit. Most real systems use a hybrid: RBAC for broad permissions, ABAC for fine-grained rules. AWS IAM is an ABAC system.

cards.question: What is an IDOR vulnerability? Give an example and explain how to prevent it.
cards.answer: IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) occurs when an application exposes internal IDs in URLs or APIs without verifying the requesting user's authorization. Example: `GET /api/invoices/12345` returns invoice 12345 — an attacker changes it to `12346` and sees another user's invoice. This is OWASP A01:2021 (Broken Access Control). Prevention: (1) always check that the authenticated user owns or has access to the requested resource (`WHERE invoice_id = $1 AND user_id = $2`), (2) use UUIDs instead of sequential IDs to prevent enumeration (but this is defense-in-depth, not a fix), (3) implement authorization middleware that runs on every endpoint, (4) use indirect references that map to real IDs server-side.

cards.question: Explain symmetric vs asymmetric encryption. When would you use AES-256-GCM vs RSA?
cards.answer: Symmetric encryption uses one shared key for encrypt and decrypt (AES, ChaCha20) — fast, used for bulk data. Asymmetric uses a key pair: public key encrypts, private key decrypts (RSA, ECDSA) — slow, used for key exchange and signatures. AES-256-GCM provides authenticated encryption (confidentiality + integrity + authenticity) with a 256-bit key and is the standard for encrypting data at rest and in transit. RSA is used to encrypt small payloads (like a symmetric key) or for digital signatures. TLS combines both: RSA/ECDHE for the handshake (key exchange), then AES-GCM for the session. Never use RSA to encrypt bulk data directly — it's 1000x slower and limited to key-size payloads.

cards.question: How does TLS 1.3 work? What improvements does it have over TLS 1.2?
cards.answer: TLS 1.3 establishes an encrypted connection in 1 round-trip (1-RTT) vs 2-RTT in TLS 1.2, with 0-RTT resumption for repeat connections. Key improvements: (1) removed insecure algorithms — no RSA key exchange (forward secrecy mandatory via ECDHE), no CBC mode, no RC4, no SHA-1; (2) simplified cipher suites from 37 to 5 (e.g., TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384); (3) handshake is encrypted after ServerHello (TLS 1.2 was plaintext until Finished); (4) removed renegotiation and compression (CRIME attack). The handshake: Client sends ClientHello + key share, Server responds with ServerHello + key share + encrypted certificate + Finished — data can flow immediately. 0-RTT has replay risk and should only be used for idempotent requests.

cards.question: What are the security concerns with JWTs? How should you handle JWT signing and validation?
cards.answer: JWT pitfalls: (1) `alg: none` attack — if the server accepts unsigned tokens, attacker removes the signature; always validate the `alg` header against an allowlist. (2) RS256/HS256 confusion — if a server expects RS256 but accepts HS256, an attacker can sign with the public key as the HMAC secret. (3) JWTs can't be revoked once issued — use short expiry (15min) + refresh tokens. (4) Sensitive data in payload is only base64-encoded, not encrypted — don't put secrets in claims. Best practices: use RS256 or EdDSA for signing, validate `iss`, `aud`, `exp`, `nbf` claims, store refresh tokens server-side (database), never store JWTs in localStorage (XSS-accessible), and use `httpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict` cookies.

cards.question: What is Content Security Policy (CSP) and how does it mitigate XSS?
cards.answer: CSP is an HTTP response header that tells the browser which sources are allowed to load resources. Example: `Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'nonce-abc123'; style-src 'self'; img-src 'self' data:; connect-src 'self' api.example.com`. This blocks inline scripts (no `<script>alert(1)</script>`), external scripts from unauthorized domains, and `eval()`. Nonce-based CSP allows specific inline scripts with a per-request random nonce. CSP also prevents data exfiltration by restricting `connect-src`. `report-uri` or `report-to` directives send violation reports to your monitoring. Start with `Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only` to test without breaking the site. CSP is defense-in-depth — it doesn't replace output encoding.

cards.question: What is the STRIDE threat model? Walk through applying it to a web application.
cards.answer: STRIDE is a Microsoft threat classification framework: Spoofing (impersonating a user/system), Tampering (modifying data), Repudiation (denying actions without accountability), Information Disclosure (data leaks), Denial of Service (making system unavailable), Elevation of Privilege (gaining unauthorized access). Applied to a web app: Spoofing — session hijacking, weak auth → mitigate with MFA, secure cookies. Tampering — modifying API payloads → mitigate with input validation, signed tokens. Repudiation — user denies action → mitigate with audit logs, non-repudiation signatures. Information Disclosure — error stack traces, verbose APIs → mitigate with generic errors, field-level access control. DoS — unthrottled endpoints → mitigate with rate limiting. EoP — IDOR, missing authz checks → mitigate with RBAC. You apply STRIDE to each component in a data flow diagram.

cards.question: What is the difference between SAST and DAST? Where do they fit in a CI/CD pipeline?
cards.answer: SAST (Static Application Security Testing) analyzes source code without executing it — finds vulnerabilities like hardcoded secrets, SQL injection patterns, insecure deserialization. Tools: Semgrep, CodeQL, SonarQube. Runs early (on every PR/commit). DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing) tests the running application by sending real HTTP requests — finds misconfigurations, auth bypasses, CORS issues. Tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Nuclei. Runs on deployed staging environments. SAST is fast but produces false positives and can't find runtime issues. DAST finds real exploitable bugs but is slower and can't point to the exact code line. Best practice: SAST in CI on every push, DAST nightly on staging, plus SCA (Software Composition Analysis) for dependency vulnerabilities (Snyk, Dependabot).

cards.question: What is an SBOM and why does supply chain security matter? Explain the SLSA framework.
cards.answer: An SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) is a machine-readable inventory of every component, library, and dependency in your software — like a nutrition label. It enables vulnerability tracking (when log4shell hits, you can instantly check if you're affected). Formats: SPDX, CycloneDX. Supply chain attacks target the build/distribution pipeline: compromised npm packages (event-stream), poisoned CI pipelines, typosquatting. SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts, pronounced 'salsa') is a Google-led framework with 4 levels: L1 (documented build process), L2 (hosted build + signed provenance), L3 (hardened builds, isolated environment), L4 (hermetic, reproducible builds with two-party review). Tools: Sigstore/cosign for artifact signing, npm provenance, GitHub artifact attestations.

cards.question: How would you securely store API keys and secrets in a production application?
cards.answer: Never hardcode secrets in source code or environment files committed to git. Use a secrets manager: AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GCP Secret Manager, or Azure Key Vault. These provide: encrypted storage, access audit logs, automatic rotation, and fine-grained IAM policies. For local development, use `.env` files (in `.gitignore`) with `dotenv`. In CI/CD, use platform-native secrets (GitHub Actions secrets, GitLab CI variables). For Kubernetes, use sealed-secrets or external-secrets-operator that syncs from Vault. Additional hardening: encrypt secrets at rest with a KMS key, rotate credentials on a schedule, use short-lived tokens where possible (AWS STS, OAuth tokens), and scan repos with tools like truffleHog or gitleaks to catch accidental commits.

cards.question: What is Broken Access Control (OWASP A01:2021) and why is it the #1 vulnerability?
cards.answer: Broken Access Control means users can act outside their intended permissions. It's #1 because it's pervasive and high-impact. Common patterns: IDOR (accessing other users' data by changing IDs), missing function-level access control (admin endpoints accessible to regular users), metadata manipulation (changing `role: admin` in a JWT), CORS misconfiguration allowing unauthorized origins, path traversal (`../../etc/passwd`), and privilege escalation through API parameter tampering. Prevention requires defense-in-depth: deny by default (explicit allow, not explicit deny), enforce authorization server-side on every request (not just UI hiding), implement centralized authorization middleware, use integration tests that verify forbidden access returns 403, and audit access logs for anomalies.

cards.question: Design a secure session management system. What are the key decisions around session storage, expiry, and invalidation?
cards.answer: Key decisions: (1) Session ID generation — use `crypto.randomBytes(32)` minimum 128 bits of entropy, never sequential or predictable. (2) Storage — server-side (Redis/DB) with session ID in an `httpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax` cookie, NOT localStorage. (3) Expiry — idle timeout (15-30 min for sensitive apps) + absolute timeout (8-24 hours) regardless of activity. (4) Invalidation — delete server-side on logout, invalidate all sessions on password change, allow users to view/revoke active sessions. (5) Rotation — regenerate session ID after privilege elevation (login, role change) to prevent session fixation. (6) Fingerprinting — bind session to User-Agent or IP range as anomaly detection. (7) Concurrent session limits — alert or block when same user has sessions from multiple countries.

cards.question: What happens during a TLS certificate validation? What is certificate pinning and why is it controversial?
cards.answer: TLS certificate validation: (1) check the certificate is not expired (`notBefore` to `notAfter`), (2) verify the chain of trust — the leaf cert is signed by an intermediate CA, which is signed by a root CA in the browser's trust store, (3) check the Subject Alternative Name (SAN) matches the hostname, (4) check revocation via CRL or OCSP (often skipped for performance — OCSP stapling helps). Certificate pinning hardcodes expected certificate fingerprints or public keys in the client — if the server presents a different cert (even a valid one), the connection is rejected. This prevents CA compromise attacks but is controversial because: certificate rotation breaks pinned clients, it caused major outages (e.g., at app companies), and Google deprecated HPKP in Chrome. Modern alternative: Certificate Transparency logs.

cards.question: What is the attack surface of a typical web application? How do you systematically reduce it?
cards.answer: Attack surface includes: (1) Network — open ports, exposed services, unencrypted endpoints → minimize with firewalls, close unnecessary ports, TLS everywhere. (2) Application — input fields, file uploads, APIs, WebSockets → validate all input, whitelist file types, rate limit endpoints. (3) Authentication — login forms, password reset, MFA enrollment → use proven libraries, enforce strong passwords, rate limit attempts. (4) Dependencies — npm/pip packages, Docker base images → SCA scanning, lock files, minimal base images. (5) Infrastructure — cloud IAM, storage bucket permissions, metadata endpoints → principle of least privilege, private subnets, IMDSv2. (6) Human — phishing, social engineering → security training, hardware MFA. Systematic reduction: inventory all entry points with a data flow diagram, classify by risk, apply STRIDE to each, prioritize by likelihood x impact, and continuously monitor with DAST + log analysis.

cards.question: What is insecure deserialization and how can it lead to remote code execution?
cards.answer: Insecure deserialization (OWASP A08:2021) occurs when an application deserializes untrusted data that contains malicious objects. In Java, `ObjectInputStream.readObject()` can instantiate arbitrary classes — an attacker crafts a serialized payload using gadget chains (e.g., Apache Commons Collections) that execute system commands during deserialization. In Python, `pickle.loads()` can execute arbitrary code via `__reduce__`. In Node.js, `node-serialize` had an RCE via `_$$ND_FUNC$$_` syntax. Impact: remote code execution, privilege escalation. Mitigations: (1) never deserialize untrusted input, (2) use safe formats like JSON (no code execution), (3) if you must use native serialization, implement allowlists of permitted classes, (4) use integrity checks (HMAC) on serialized data, (5) run deserialization in sandboxed environments with minimal privileges.

cards.question: How would you implement rate limiting to protect an API? Compare token bucket, sliding window, and fixed window algorithms.
cards.answer: Fixed window: count requests in discrete time windows (e.g., 100 req/min). Simple but has burst problem at window boundaries — a user can send 100 requests at 0:59 and 100 at 1:00. Sliding window log: tracks each request timestamp, counts requests in the last N seconds. Accurate but memory-intensive. Sliding window counter: hybrid — uses current + previous window counts weighted by overlap percentage. Good accuracy with low memory. Token bucket: a bucket holds tokens (max = burst size), refilled at a steady rate. Each request consumes a token. Allows controlled bursts while enforcing sustained rate. This is what AWS API Gateway and nginx use. Implementation: use Redis with `INCR` + `EXPIRE` for distributed rate limiting, or `EVALSHA` for atomic sliding window. Apply per-user, per-IP, and per-endpoint limits. Return `429 Too Many Requests` with `Retry-After` header.

cards.question: What are the properties of a Red-Black tree, and why is it preferred over a plain BST?
cards.answer: A Red-Black tree is a self-balancing BST with these properties: (1) every node is red or black, (2) the root is black, (3) all leaves (NIL) are black, (4) red nodes cannot have red children, (5) every path from a node to its descendant leaves has the same number of black nodes. This guarantees O(log n) height. It is preferred over plain BSTs because unbalanced BSTs can degrade to O(n) for search/insert/delete. Red-Black trees are used in Java's TreeMap, C++ std::map, and Linux's CFS scheduler. Compared to AVL trees, Red-Black trees do fewer rotations on insert/delete, making them better for write-heavy workloads.

cards.question: What is a B-tree, and why is it the standard data structure for database indexes?
cards.answer: A B-tree of order m is a self-balancing search tree where each node can have up to m children and m-1 keys. All leaves are at the same depth, and internal nodes are at least half-full. B-trees are ideal for database indexes because: (1) they minimize disk I/O by having a high branching factor (each node fits a disk page, typically 4-16KB), (2) a B-tree with branching factor 1000 stores 1 billion keys in only 3 levels, meaning 3 disk reads for any lookup, (3) range queries are efficient since leaf nodes can be linked. A B+ tree variant stores all data in leaves with a linked list, making sequential scans even faster. PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, and SQLite all use B+ trees for their indexes.

cards.question: What is a Trie, and how would you implement autocomplete with one?
cards.answer: A Trie (prefix tree) is a tree where each edge represents a character and paths from root to marked nodes represent stored strings. For autocomplete: (1) build a trie from the dictionary, (2) given a prefix, traverse the trie following the prefix characters, (3) from the terminal node, do a DFS/BFS to collect all words below it. Time complexity is O(p + k) where p is prefix length and k is total characters in all results. Space optimization: use a compressed trie (radix tree) that merges single-child chains into one edge. For ranking, store frequency counts at terminal nodes and use a priority queue during collection. Real-world systems like search engines add a ternary search trie or combine with an inverted index for fuzzy matching.

cards.question: What is a Segment Tree and what problems does it solve?
cards.answer: A Segment Tree is a binary tree that stores aggregate information (sum, min, max, GCD) over intervals of an array. Each leaf represents a single element; each internal node represents the merge of its children's intervals. It supports both point updates and range queries in O(log n). Construction is O(n). Key applications: range sum/min/max queries with updates, counting elements in a range, interval scheduling. Lazy propagation extends it to support range updates in O(log n) by deferring updates to child nodes until they are queried. Compared to a Fenwick tree, segment trees are more versatile (support any associative operation, not just invertible ones like addition) but use 2-4x more memory.

cards.question: What is a Fenwick Tree (Binary Indexed Tree) and when would you choose it over a Segment Tree?
cards.answer: A Fenwick Tree (BIT) is a compact array-based structure that supports prefix sum queries and point updates in O(log n) with O(n) space. It works by storing partial sums at indices determined by the lowest set bit of the index. To query prefix sum(i), you sum values at i, i - LSB(i), i - LSB(i) - LSB(...), etc. Choose a Fenwick tree over a Segment Tree when: (1) you only need prefix/range sums (or any invertible operation like XOR), (2) you want minimal memory (just one array of size n+1 vs 4n for segment tree), (3) simpler implementation matters. Fenwick trees cannot handle non-invertible operations like range-min without modifications. They are commonly used in competitive programming for inversion counting and 2D prefix sums.

cards.question: What is a Bloom filter and what are its trade-offs?
cards.answer: A Bloom filter is a probabilistic data structure that tests set membership using a bit array of m bits and k hash functions. Insert: hash the element k times, set those bit positions to 1. Query: check if all k positions are 1. It has false positives (says 'maybe in set') but never false negatives (if it says 'not in set', that is guaranteed). Trade-offs: extremely space-efficient (10 bits per element gives ~1% false positive rate), O(k) insert and query. Drawbacks: cannot delete elements (use a Counting Bloom Filter for that), false positive rate grows as the filter fills. Used in: Chrome's safe browsing (check URLs against malware list), Cassandra/HBase (skip reading SSTables that definitely don't contain a key), CDNs (avoid caching one-hit wonders).

cards.question: What is HyperLogLog and how does it estimate cardinality?
cards.answer: HyperLogLog (HLL) estimates the number of distinct elements in a dataset using only O(log log n) memory. It works by hashing each element and observing the position of the leftmost 1-bit in the hash. The intuition: if you have seen a hash with k leading zeros, you have likely seen ~2^k distinct elements. HLL improves accuracy by splitting hashes into m buckets (using first p bits as bucket index), tracking the max leading-zero count per bucket, then computing the harmonic mean across buckets. With 2^14 = 16384 buckets (12KB memory), it achieves ~0.81% standard error. Used in Redis (PFADD/PFCOUNT), BigQuery (APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT), and analytics systems to count unique visitors, unique queries, etc.

cards.question: What is a Skip List and how does it achieve O(log n) search?
cards.answer: A Skip List is a probabilistic alternative to balanced trees. It is a layered linked list where the bottom layer contains all elements in sorted order, and each higher layer is a subset (each element is promoted with probability 1/2). Searching starts at the top layer and moves right until overshooting, then drops down one layer and continues. Expected search time is O(log n) because there are O(log n) layers and O(1) expected steps per layer. Insert: search for position, insert at bottom layer, then flip a coin to decide promotion to each higher layer. Advantages over balanced trees: simpler implementation, naturally supports concurrent access (lock-striping per tower), efficient range queries. Redis uses skip lists for sorted sets (ZRANGEBYSCORE). Space is O(n) expected.

cards.question: What is a Count-Min Sketch and how does it differ from a Bloom filter?
cards.answer: A Count-Min Sketch (CMS) is a probabilistic data structure for frequency estimation in streaming data. It uses a 2D array of d rows x w columns with d independent hash functions. To increment: hash the item with each function, increment the corresponding cell in each row. To query frequency: take the minimum across all d rows. CMS can overestimate but never underestimate frequencies. Unlike a Bloom filter (which answers 'is this element in the set?'), CMS answers 'how many times has this element appeared?' Both use multiple hash functions and have one-sided errors. CMS is used in network traffic analysis (heavy hitter detection), NLP (word frequency estimation), and database query optimization (join size estimation). Space is O(d * w), independent of stream length.

cards.question: How does the Ford-Fulkerson method solve the Maximum Flow problem?
cards.answer: Ford-Fulkerson finds the maximum flow in a flow network by repeatedly finding augmenting paths from source to sink in the residual graph and pushing flow along them. The residual graph has forward edges (remaining capacity) and backward edges (flow that can be reversed). Algorithm: (1) initialize all flows to 0, (2) while an augmenting path exists in the residual graph, find the bottleneck capacity along the path and augment flow by that amount, (3) update residual capacities. The Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem guarantees this equals the minimum cut. The basic DFS approach can be slow (O(E * max_flow)) with integer capacities. Edmonds-Karp uses BFS to find shortest augmenting paths, giving O(V * E^2). Dinic's algorithm achieves O(V^2 * E) using level graphs and blocking flows. Applications: bipartite matching, network routing, baseball elimination.

cards.question: Compare Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms for finding a Minimum Spanning Tree.
cards.answer: Both find the MST of a weighted, connected, undirected graph. Kruskal's: sort all edges by weight, greedily add the lightest edge that does not form a cycle (using Union-Find to detect cycles). Time: O(E log E). Works well for sparse graphs. Prim's: grow the MST from an arbitrary start vertex, always adding the cheapest edge connecting a tree vertex to a non-tree vertex (using a min-heap of crossing edges). Time: O(E log V) with a binary heap, O(E + V log V) with a Fibonacci heap. Works well for dense graphs. Both produce optimal MSTs (by the cut property). Kruskal's is simpler to implement and doesn't need adjacency list representation. Prim's can be more efficient on dense graphs and naturally produces the tree by growing from a single component.

cards.question: What is Tarjan's algorithm and what does it find?
cards.answer: Tarjan's algorithm finds all Strongly Connected Components (SCCs) in a directed graph in O(V + E) using a single DFS pass. A SCC is a maximal set of vertices where every vertex is reachable from every other. The algorithm maintains a DFS stack and two arrays: disc[] (discovery time) and low[] (lowest discovery time reachable via back edges). When DFS completes a vertex, if its low value equals its disc value, it is the root of a SCC. Pop all vertices from the stack up to and including this root to form the SCC. Applications: (1) condensing a directed graph into a DAG of SCCs for topological analysis, (2) solving 2-SAT problems, (3) finding cycles in dependency graphs, (4) computing dominator trees in compilers. An alternative is Kosaraju's algorithm (two DFS passes), which is simpler but slightly slower in practice.

cards.question: How does A* search work and why is it optimal?
cards.answer: A* is a best-first search algorithm that finds the shortest path from start to goal by using f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where g(n) is the actual cost from start to n, and h(n) is a heuristic estimate from n to the goal. It maintains an open set (priority queue by f-value) and a closed set of visited nodes. A* is optimal (finds shortest path) if the heuristic is admissible (never overestimates the true cost) and consistent (h(n) <= cost(n, n') + h(n')). Common heuristics: Manhattan distance for grids without diagonals, Euclidean distance for free movement, Chebyshev distance for grids with diagonals. A* degenerates to Dijkstra's when h = 0, and to greedy best-first when g = 0. Time complexity is O(b^d) worst case where b is branching factor and d is depth, but a good heuristic dramatically prunes the search space. Used in game pathfinding, robot navigation, and route planning.

cards.question: What is the KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) string matching algorithm and how does its failure function work?
cards.answer: KMP searches for a pattern P of length m in a text T of length n in O(n + m) time, compared to naive O(n * m). The key insight is precomputing a failure function (partial match table / prefix function) for the pattern. The failure function lps[i] stores the length of the longest proper prefix of P[0..i] that is also a suffix. During matching, when a mismatch occurs at P[j] after matching j characters, instead of restarting from the next text position, KMP shifts the pattern so that P[0..lps[j-1]-1] aligns with the already-matched suffix, and continues from P[lps[j-1]]. This avoids re-examining text characters. Example: pattern 'ABABC' has lps = [0,0,1,2,0]. If mismatch at index 4 (C), we know 'AB' at the end matches 'AB' at the start, so we resume matching from index 2. Building the failure function itself uses the same shift logic in O(m).

cards.question: How does Rabin-Karp string matching work and when would you use it over KMP?
cards.answer: Rabin-Karp uses rolling hash functions to find pattern matches. Compute the hash of the pattern and the hash of each text window of the same length. If hashes match, verify character-by-character to rule out hash collisions. The rolling hash updates in O(1): when sliding the window one position right, subtract the contribution of the leftmost character and add the new rightmost character. Typical hash: H = (c0 * d^(m-1) + c1 * d^(m-2) + ... + cm-1) mod q, where d is alphabet size and q is a large prime. Average time: O(n + m). Worst case: O(nm) if many hash collisions. Choose Rabin-Karp over KMP when: (1) you need to search for multiple patterns simultaneously (compute one hash per window, check against a set of pattern hashes), (2) 2D pattern matching (extend rolling hash to rows and columns), (3) plagiarism detection (compare document fingerprints). KMP is better for single-pattern matching with guaranteed O(n+m).

cards.question: What is a Suffix Array and how does it compare to a Suffix Tree?
cards.answer: A Suffix Array is a sorted array of all suffixes of a string, stored as indices. For 'banana$', suffixes sorted: ['$','a$','ana$','anana$','banana$','na$','nana$'], so SA = [6,5,3,1,0,4,2]. With an LCP (Longest Common Prefix) array, it supports substring search in O(m log n) via binary search, longest repeated substring in O(n), and counting occurrences efficiently. Construction: naive is O(n^2 log n), but DC3/SA-IS algorithms build it in O(n). A Suffix Tree stores the same information as a tree structure, supporting O(m) substring search but using 10-20x more memory. In practice, suffix arrays with LCP arrays are preferred because they are cache-friendly, use less memory (O(n) integers vs O(n) nodes with pointers), and support the same operations. Used in bioinformatics (genome alignment), data compression (BWT), and full-text search engines.

cards.question: What is a Fibonacci Heap and why is it theoretically faster for Dijkstra's algorithm?
cards.answer: A Fibonacci Heap is a collection of heap-ordered trees with lazy consolidation. Key operations: insert O(1) amortized, find-min O(1), decrease-key O(1) amortized, delete-min O(log n) amortized, merge O(1). The O(1) decrease-key is what makes it theoretically superior for Dijkstra's algorithm: Dijkstra's does O(V) delete-min and O(E) decrease-key operations, giving O(V log V + E) with Fibonacci heaps vs O((V + E) log V) with binary heaps. This matters when E >> V (dense graphs). However, Fibonacci heaps are rarely used in practice because: (1) high constant factors from complex node management, (2) poor cache performance due to pointer-heavy structure, (3) binary heaps and pairing heaps are simpler and faster for real-world graph sizes. The two-heaps interview pattern (max-heap + min-heap for running median) uses binary heaps, not Fibonacci heaps.

cards.question: How would you design a system to find the running median of a stream of integers?
cards.answer: Use the two-heaps pattern: maintain a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half. Invariant: max-heap size equals min-heap size (even total) or is one larger (odd total). To add a number: (1) if it is less than or equal to the max-heap's top, push to max-heap; otherwise push to min-heap, (2) rebalance: if max-heap has more than one extra element, move its top to min-heap; if min-heap is larger, move its top to max-heap. Median: if equal sizes, average the two tops; otherwise, max-heap's top. Insert is O(log n), query is O(1). Alternative approaches: a balanced BST (like an order-statistic tree) gives O(log n) for both insert and median via rank queries. For approximate median in distributed systems, use quantile sketches like t-digest or Q-digest.

cards.question: What is Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) and how do path compression and union by rank optimize it?
cards.answer: Union-Find tracks a collection of disjoint sets supporting two operations: Find(x) returns the representative (root) of x's set, and Union(x, y) merges the sets containing x and y. Naive implementation uses a parent array where Find follows parent pointers to the root. Path compression: during Find, make every node on the path point directly to the root, flattening the tree. Union by rank: always attach the shorter tree under the taller tree's root, keeping trees balanced. With both optimizations, any sequence of m operations on n elements runs in O(m * alpha(n)) where alpha is the inverse Ackermann function, effectively O(1) amortized per operation. Applications: Kruskal's MST algorithm (cycle detection), connected components in dynamic graphs, percolation problems, equivalence class grouping, and network connectivity queries.

cards.question: What is Bitmask DP and how would you apply it to the Traveling Salesman Problem?
cards.answer: Bitmask DP uses a bitmask integer to represent subsets of elements as DP states. For TSP with n cities: dp[mask][i] = minimum cost to visit exactly the cities in 'mask', ending at city i. Base case: dp[1 << start][start] = 0. Transition: for each state (mask, i), try extending to unvisited city j: dp[mask | (1 << j)][j] = min(dp[mask][j], dp[mask][i] + dist[i][j]). Answer: min over all i of dp[(1 << n) - 1][i] + dist[i][start]. Time: O(2^n * n^2), space: O(2^n * n). This is exponential but much better than the O(n!) brute force. Practical for n <= 20-22. Bitmask DP is also used for: assignment problems, Hamiltonian paths, set cover, and matching problems. The key insight is that a bitmask of size n encodes which elements have been used, turning subset enumeration into integer arithmetic.

cards.question: What is Digit DP and how would you count numbers with specific digit properties in a range?
cards.answer: Digit DP is a technique for counting integers in [0, N] satisfying some digit-based property (e.g., sum of digits = k, no repeated digits, divisible by m). You process the number digit by digit from most significant to least significant. State: dp[position][tight][...problem-specific state]. 'tight' is a boolean indicating whether previous digits exactly match N's prefix (constraining the current digit to [0, N[pos]]) or are already smaller (allowing [0, 9]). For counting numbers without repeated digits up to N: state is dp[pos][tight][used_digits_mask]. At each position, try each digit d not in used_mask; if tight and d < N[pos], future positions become untight. Time is O(digits * 2 * 2^10 * 10) for decimal digits with the used-mask approach. Digit DP is common in competitive programming and appears in interview problems like 'count numbers with at most k non-zero digits in range [L, R]'.

cards.question: What is Tree DP and how would you find the diameter of a tree?
cards.answer: Tree DP solves optimization problems on trees by rooting the tree and computing DP values bottom-up from leaves to root. For tree diameter (longest path between any two nodes): root at any node, then for each node u, compute dp[u] = the length of the longest path starting at u going downward. The diameter passing through u is dp[u_child1] + dp[u_child2] (sum of two longest downward paths from u's children plus 2 edges). Track the global maximum across all nodes. This runs in O(n). Other Tree DP applications: (1) maximum independent set on a tree (dp[v][taken/not_taken]), (2) counting subtree sizes, (3) rerooting technique where you compute the answer for every possible root in O(n) total by first solving for one root, then propagating changes when moving the root to an adjacent node. Tree DP frequently appears in interviews for problems on organizational hierarchies, file systems, and network topologies.

cards.question: What is the difference between a process and a thread?
cards.answer: A process is an independent execution unit with its own memory space (virtual address space, file descriptors, heap). A thread is a lightweight execution unit within a process that shares the process's memory and resources but has its own stack and program counter. Key differences: (1) memory isolation: processes are isolated (crash in one does not affect another); threads share memory (a rogue pointer can corrupt another thread's data), (2) creation cost: processes are expensive to create (fork copies page tables); threads are cheaper (just allocate a stack), (3) communication: inter-process communication (IPC) requires pipes, sockets, or shared memory; threads communicate via shared variables (but need synchronization), (4) context switching: thread switches are faster since no TLB flush or page table switch is needed. Modern systems use threads for parallelism within an application and processes for isolation between applications.

cards.question: What are green threads, goroutines, and virtual threads, and how do they differ from OS threads?
cards.answer: All three are user-space or runtime-managed lightweight threads multiplexed onto fewer OS threads. Green threads (e.g., early Java, Erlang processes): scheduled by the language runtime, not the OS kernel. Cheap to create (KB-sized stacks vs MB for OS threads), but blocking one blocks the underlying OS thread unless the runtime has an I/O scheduler. Goroutines (Go): M:N threading model where the Go runtime schedules goroutines onto a pool of OS threads (GOMAXPROCS). Start with 2-8KB stacks that grow dynamically. The runtime handles I/O by parking goroutines and using netpoller (epoll/kqueue). Virtual Threads (Java 21+, Project Loom): platform-managed lightweight threads that unmount from carrier OS threads during blocking operations (I/O, locks). Allow writing simple blocking code that scales like async code. Key advantage of all three: you can have millions of concurrent tasks vs thousands of OS threads, because OS thread stack size (1-8MB) and kernel scheduling overhead limit scalability.

cards.question: What is a mutex and how does it differ from a semaphore?
cards.answer: A mutex (mutual exclusion) is a binary lock: only one thread can hold it at a time. A thread acquires the lock before entering a critical section and releases it when done. It has ownership semantics (only the thread that locked it can unlock it). A semaphore is a counter-based signaling mechanism: it maintains a count and allows up to N threads to proceed (counting semaphore) or just 1 (binary semaphore). Any thread can signal (increment) a semaphore, regardless of which thread waited on it. Key differences: (1) ownership: mutex has it, semaphore does not, (2) purpose: mutex protects shared data, semaphore controls access to a finite pool of resources (e.g., connection pool of size 10) or signals between threads (producer-consumer), (3) priority inversion: mutexes can implement priority inheritance (boosting holder's priority), semaphores typically cannot. In practice, use a mutex for critical sections and a semaphore (or condition variable) for signaling and resource counting.

cards.question: What is a barrier in concurrent programming and when would you use one?
cards.answer: A barrier is a synchronization primitive where a group of threads all wait until every thread in the group has reached the barrier point, then all proceed together. Think of it as a checkpoint where nobody moves forward until everyone arrives. Implementation: maintain a counter and a condition variable. Each arriving thread increments the counter; if count < N, it waits on the condition variable. The Nth thread to arrive broadcasts the condition, releasing all waiters. Use cases: (1) parallel iterative computations where each phase must complete before the next begins (e.g., parallel matrix relaxation, physics simulations), (2) parallel testing where you want all threads to start hammering a resource simultaneously (stress testing), (3) parallel prefix/reduction operations between phases. In Java, CyclicBarrier and Phaser provide this. Pthreads has pthread_barrier_t. Go's sync.WaitGroup serves a similar role for fork-join patterns.

cards.question: What is a Compare-And-Swap (CAS) operation and why is it fundamental to lock-free programming?
cards.answer: CAS is an atomic CPU instruction that compares a memory location's current value to an expected value, and only if they match, swaps in a new value. It returns whether the swap succeeded. Pseudocode: CAS(addr, expected, new) { if (*addr == expected) { *addr = new; return true; } return false; }. CAS is the building block of lock-free programming because it allows threads to make progress without holding locks. Pattern: read the current value, compute the desired new value, attempt CAS. If it fails (another thread modified the value), retry (spin). This is wait-free for the winning thread and lock-free overall (at least one thread always makes progress). CAS is used to implement: atomic counters, lock-free stacks (Treiber stack), lock-free queues (Michael-Scott queue), and concurrent hash maps. The ABA problem is a pitfall: if a value changes A->B->A, CAS succeeds incorrectly. Solved with version counters or hazard pointers.

cards.question: What does 'happens-before' mean in the Java Memory Model?
cards.answer: In the Java Memory Model (JMM), 'happens-before' is a partial ordering of actions that guarantees visibility: if action A happens-before action B, then A's effects are visible to B. Key happens-before rules: (1) program order: each action in a thread happens-before the next action in that thread, (2) monitor lock: unlocking a monitor happens-before the subsequent locking of that same monitor, (3) volatile: a write to a volatile field happens-before every subsequent read of that field, (4) thread start: Thread.start() happens-before any action in the started thread, (5) thread join: all actions in a thread happen-before Thread.join() returns, (6) transitivity: if A happens-before B and B happens-before C, then A happens-before C. Without happens-before, the compiler, JIT, CPU, and cache hierarchy can reorder operations, causing threads to see stale or partially-written values. This is why unsynchronized access to shared mutable state is a bug even if it 'seems to work' in testing.

cards.question: What are acquire and release memory ordering semantics?
cards.answer: Acquire and release are memory ordering constraints used in lock-free programming. An acquire operation (e.g., loading a flag) ensures that all memory reads/writes after it in program order cannot be reordered before it. A release operation (e.g., storing a flag) ensures that all memory reads/writes before it cannot be reordered after it. Together, they create a synchronization pair: a release-store in thread A paired with an acquire-load of the same location in thread B ensures that everything A wrote before the release is visible to B after the acquire. This is weaker (and faster) than sequential consistency (which orders all operations globally) but sufficient for most synchronization patterns. In C++: std::memory_order_acquire and std::memory_order_release. In Rust: Ordering::Acquire and Ordering::Release. Real-world use: implementing a spinlock (acquire on lock, release on unlock) or publishing data via a flag variable.

cards.question: How does the reactor pattern work, and how does it differ from the proactor pattern?
cards.answer: The reactor pattern is an event-driven I/O multiplexing model: a single event loop (reactor) monitors multiple file descriptors using select/poll/epoll/kqueue. When a descriptor becomes ready for I/O, the reactor dispatches the event to the appropriate handler. The handler then performs the I/O operation synchronously (non-blocking read/write). Used in: Node.js (libuv), Nginx, Redis, Netty. The proactor pattern inverts the model: the OS or framework performs the I/O operation asynchronously, and when it completes, it notifies the application via a completion callback. The handler receives already-completed data rather than a 'ready' signal. Used in: Windows IOCP, Boost.Asio, io_uring on Linux.
